blocks:
Ayahs

تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ نَذِيرًا (١)
Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant that he may be a warner to all the nations. (1)
ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ شَرِيكٌ فِى ٱلْمُلْكِ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُۥ تَقْدِيرًا (٢)
He, to whom belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth, and who did not take any offspring, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, and He created everything and determined it in a precise measure. (2)
وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً لَّا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتًا وَلَا حَيَوٰةً وَلَا نُشُورًا (٣)
Yet they have taken gods besides Him who create nothing and have themselves been created, and who have no control over their own harm or benefit and have no control over [their own] death, life, or resurrection. (3)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to clarify that the call of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a truthful call arising from a divine message on behalf of Allah, the Exalted, and a Book revealed from Him. It pays profound attention to refuting the objections raised by the disbelievers (الكفار) regarding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being a messenger from Allah and his Book being revealed from Him, returning to this theme time and again.

This objective entails some argumentation for monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the negation of any partner (الشريك), as well as mentioning some descriptions of the Day of Resurrection and a brief account of the beautiful attributes of the believers (المؤمنين). The discourse therein follows the style of warning (الإنذار) and instilling fear rather than glad tidings.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of the vast majority of its verses. However, three verses are sometimes excepted, namely His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ لا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ — "Those who do not invoke another deity besides Allah..." until His saying: غَفُوراً رَحِيماً — "...All-forgiving, All-merciful." Perhaps the reason for this exception is that they contain the legislation of the prohibition of adultery (الزنا). However, you have already learned from what we cited regarding the reports on the verse of wine in Surah al-Ma'idah that adultery and wine were known to be prohibited in Islam from the very beginning of the Islamic call.

Strangely, some have claimed that the entire Surah is Medinan except for the first three verses: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي — "Blessed is He..." until His saying: نُشُوراً — "...resurrection."

His saying, the Exalted: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً — Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant that he may be a warner to all the worlds. (1)

Blessing (البركة) refers to the permanence of good in a thing, similar to the settling of water in a pool (البركة). It is derived from the verb "the camel knelt" (برك البعير) when it places its chest upon the ground and settles there. From this comes tabaraka (تبارك) in the sense of the permanence of abundant good. The form of the word indicates intensification (المبالغة), as has been said. It is almost exclusively used for Allah, the Exalted, and is not applied to others except rarely.

The Criterion (الفرقان) — the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood — is the "distinction" (الفرق). The Quran is named thus because its verses were revealed separately (متفرقة), or because it distinguishes truth from falsehood. This latter meaning is supported by the application of the term "Criterion" (الفرقان) in His speech, the Exalted, to the Torah as well, despite it being revealed all at once. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The Criterion (الفرقان) is more emphatic than 'distinction' (الفرق) because it is used for the distinction between truth and falsehood... it is a noun, not a verbal noun (مصدر) according to what has been said, whereas 'distinction' (الفرق) is used for that and for other things." End quote.

"All the worlds" (العالمون) is the plural of "world" (عالم), meaning the creation (الخلق). It is said in al-Sihah: "The world (العالم) is the creation, and the plural is al-awalim (العوالم), and al-alamun (العالمون) refers to the various types of creation." End quote. Although the term encompasses all creation—inanimate objects, plants, animals, humans, jinn, and angels—the context of the verse, which makes warning (الإنذار) the goal of the Quran's revelation, indicates that the intended meaning is the legally accountable beings (المكلفين) among the creation. These are the two weighty groups (الثقلان): humans and jinn, as far as we know.

From this, it becomes clear that the claim made by some—that the verse indicates the universality of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) message to everything other than Allah—is untenable. Such a view overlooks the reason for expressing the message in terms of "warning" (الإنذار). Similar to this verse is His saying, the Exalted: وَاصْطَفاكِ عَلى نِساءِ الْعالَمِينَ — "...and chosen you above the women of all the worlds" (3:42), and His saying: وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ — "...and preferred them over all the worlds" (45:16).

The "warner" (النذير) means the one who warns (المنذر), and "warning" (الإنذار) is close in meaning to "frightening."

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى عَبْدِهِ — "Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant" means that abundant good has been established and realized in Him who sent down the Criterion upon His servant Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The establishment of abundant good returning to the creation within Him, the Exalted, is an allusion (كناية) to its overflowing from Him upon His creation, as He revealed to His servant a Book that distinguishes between truth and falsehood, saving all the worlds from misguidance and driving them toward guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path.

The combination in the verse between the revelation of the Quran from Him, the Exalted, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being a messenger from Him and a warner to all the worlds—along with naming the Quran a "Criterion" (فرقان) between truth and falsehood and describing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as being His "servant" (عبدا) and a "warner" (نذيرا), which implies his being owned and commanded, possessing nothing of himself—all of this is a preparation for what He will later narrate regarding the polytheists (المشركين). This includes their disparagement of the Quran as a fabrication against Allah that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) invented with the help of others, and their disparagement of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) because he eats food and walks in the markets, and other things they uttered—and that which refutes their criticisms.

The summary is that it is a Book that distinguishes, by its brilliant proof, between truth and falsehood; thus, it can only be truth, for falsehood does not distinguish between truth and falsehood, but rather falsehood is made to resemble truth to deceive people. Furthermore, the one who brought it is a servant obedient to Allah, warning all the worlds with it and calling them to the truth; thus, he can only be upon the truth. Had he been a follower of falsehood, he would not have called to the truth but would have deviated and swerved from it. Moreover, Allah, the Glorified, testifies in His miraculous speech to the truthfulness of his message and that the Book he brought is revealed from Him.

From here, the weakness of the claim made by some becomes apparent: that "the Criterion" (الفرقان) refers to all the heavenly books revealed to the prophets, and "His servant" refers to all the prophets (عليه السلام). Its distance from the apparent meaning of the wording is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً — "that he may be a warner to all the worlds." The letter lam is for causality (التعليل), indicating that the purpose of sending down the Criterion upon His servant is for him to be a warner to all the worlds of humans and jinn. The plural form defined by the article al- (الجمع المحلى باللام) signifies exhaustiveness (الاستغراق). The use of this plural form is not without an indication that all have one God, unlike the view of the idolaters (الوثنيون) where each people takes a god other than what others take.

The mention of warning (الإنذار) alone, without glad tidings, is because the discourse in the Surah is directed toward warning and instilling fear.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth... (2) until the end of the verse. Ownership (الملك) — with a kasrah or fathah on the mim — is the subsistence of a thing by another such that the latter may dispose of it as he wishes. This is true whether it is the subsistence of its essence (رقبته), like the subsistence of the essence of property by its owner such that he has various types of disposal over it, or its subsistence by his dominance over it through disposal by command, prohibition, and various types of governance, like a king's dominance over his subjects and what is in their hands. The second type is often called mulk (الملك) with a dammah on the mim.

Thus, milk (الملك) — with a kasrah — is more general than mulk (الملك) — with a dammah. As al-Raghib said: "The malik (الملك) — with a fathah on the mim and kasrah on the lam — is the one who disposes by command and prohibition among the masses, and this is specific to the governance of rational beings; hence it is said 'king of the people' and not 'king of things'..." until he said: "So mulk (الملك) — with a dammah — is the control of the thing disposed of through governance, and milk (الملك) — with a kasrah — is like the genus for mulk. Every mulk (الملك) — with a dammah — is milk (الملك) — with a kasrah — but not every milk is mulk." End quote.

Sometimes milk (الملك) — with a kasrah — is restricted to what pertains to the essence (الرقبة), and mulk (الملك) — with a dammah — to other than that.

So His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth" — where the lam denotes exclusive possession (الاختصاص) — implies that the heavens and the earth are owned by Him, not independent in themselves in any of their aspects, nor self-sufficient from His disposal through governance. Governance within them and the turning of their gears is exclusive to Allah, the Exalted; He is the Sovereign (المليك) who disposes through governance over them absolutely.

By this, the connection of His saying: وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً — "...and He has not taken a son" to what preceded it becomes clear. Absolute sovereignty leaves no need for taking a son, for taking a son is for one of two reasons: either because the person is not strong enough to manage all his affairs and does not possess the management of them all, so he takes a son to assist him in some of his needs—and Allah, the Glorified, owns everything and is powerful over whatever He wills; or because the person is limited in duration, owning what he owns only for a limited term, so he takes a son to succeed him and manage his affairs after him—and Allah, the Glorified, owns everything eternally (سرمدا) and is not subject to extinction or cessation. Thus, He has no need whatsoever to take a son. In this is a refutation of the polytheists and the Christians.

Likewise, His saying after it: وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ — "...nor has He any partner in the kingdom." The need for a partner only arises when the sovereignty does not encompass all matters. But His ownership, the Exalted, is general to all things, encompassing all their aspects, with nothing excluded from it. In this is a refutation of the polytheists.

His saying, the Exalted: وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً — "...and He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure" is a clarification that the management (تدبير) of all matters returns to Him, the Exalted, alone through creation (الخلق) and ordainment (التقدير). He is the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين); there is no Lord besides Him.

The explanation of this is that since creation occurs through the mediation of causes (الأسباب) that precede the thing and accompany it, this necessitates the interconnection of the existences of things with one another. Thus, the existence of every thing and the effects of its existence are measured (يتقدر) according to what is ordained by the causes and factors preceding and accompanying it. The events occurring in the world according to the witnessed order are intertwined with creation, following the preceding and accompanying causes and factors. Since there is no creator other than Allah, the Glorified, there is no manager of affairs (مدبر للأمر) other than Him; thus, there is no Lord (رب) who owns things and manages their affairs other than Him.

The fact that He, the Exalted, possesses the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, ruling and disposing in them absolutely, necessitates that creation (الخلقة) subsists through Him; for if it subsisted through another, the kingdom would belong to that other. The subsistence of creation through Him necessitates the subsistence of ordainment (التقدير) through Him, because ordainment is a branch of creation. The subsistence of ordainment through Him necessitates the subsistence of management (التدبير) through Him. Thus, to Him belongs the kingdom and the management; He is the Lord (الرب), exalted is His state.

Although His ownership, the Exalted, of the heavens and the earth necessitates the attribution of creation and ordainment to Him, the idolaters—while admitting the generality of His ownership—believed that His ownership of all and His Lordship over the whole did not negate the ownership and Lordship of their deities over some parts, by His delegating (تفويض) that to them. Thus, each of the deities was a sovereign in the region of its divinity, a Lord for its subjects, while Allah, the Glorified, was the King of kings, the Lord of lords, and the God of gods.

Therefore, His saying: الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth" was not sufficient to prove the exclusivity of Lordship (الربوبية) to Him, the Exalted, against them. Rather, it was necessary to bring His saying: وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً — "...and He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure."

It is as if someone were to say: "Granted that His ownership of the heavens and the earth makes Him independent of taking a son or a partner—which would imply the removal of His ownership from some things—but why is it not possible for Him to take some of His creation as a partner for Himself by delegating some affairs of the world to them, while He remains the owner of them and what He delegated to them?" This is what the polytheists believed; they used to say in the talbiyah of the Hajj: "At Your service, You have no partner, except a partner who is Yours; You own him and what he owns."

So it was answered that creation belongs to Him, the Glorified, and ordainment is inseparable from it. When these two combine, management (التدبير) necessarily follows them. Thus, to Him, the Glorified, belongs the management of every thing. There is no ownership alongside His ownership, nor any Lordship alongside His Lordship.

In summary, His saying: الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ — "to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and He has not taken a son, nor has He any partner in the kingdom" is directed toward the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) and the negation of a son and a partner by way of proving absolute ownership. And His saying: وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً — "...and He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure" is an affirmation and clarification of the meaning of the generality of ownership, and that it is an ownership based upon creation and ordainment, necessitating His, the Exalted, undertaking of every judgment and management without delegating any part of the matter to anyone among the creation.

Regarding this verse and the one before it, they have other opinions which we have overlooked mentioning due to their lack of benefit.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ — They have taken besides Him gods who create nothing and are themselves created... (3) etc. When He described Himself as the Creator of everything and its Ordainer, and that to Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth—and this is how the God who is worshipped must be—He pointed to the misguidance of the polytheists who worshipped idols that do not create anything, but are themselves created and fashioned by them, possessing nothing for themselves or for others.

The pronoun in "they have taken" (وَاتَّخَذُوا) refers to the polytheists as indicated by the context, even if they were not mentioned previously. Such an expression conveys contempt and disdain.

His saying: مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ — "...besides Him gods who create nothing and are themselves created" refers to their idols which they fashioned with their own hands through carving or the like. Describing them as "gods" (آلهة) while following it with "who create nothing and are themselves created" is an indication that they have nothing of divinity except a name they have called them by, without any of its reality being realized, as He, the Exalted, said: إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْماءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ — "They are but names which you have named—you and your fathers..." (53:23).

The use of the indefinite noun in His saying: لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً — "...create nothing" in the context of negation is for emphasis in rebuking them, as they turned away from Allah, the Glorified—who is the Creator of everything—and attached themselves to idols that do not create anything at all. Rather, they are in a worse state than that, as they are fashioned by their worshippers and created by their imaginations. A similar discourse applies to His saying: ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً — "...harm or benefit" and His saying: مَوْتاً وَلا حَياةً وَلا نُشُوراً — "...death or life or resurrection."

His saying: وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً — "...and who have no control over any harm or benefit for themselves" is a negation of ownership (الملك) from them, which is essential for a God. Their worshippers only worship them to ward off harm from themselves and bring benefit to themselves. Since they do not own harm or benefit even for themselves, their worship is nothing but madness and misguidance.

From this, it becomes clear that the inclusion of "for themselves" (لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ) in the context adds to the rebuke. The speech is in the sense of progression (الترقي); that is, they do not own harm for themselves such that they could ward it off, nor benefit such that they could bring it—so how could they for others? Harm has been mentioned before benefit because warding off harm is more important than bringing benefit.

His saying: وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتاً وَلا حَياةً وَلا نُشُوراً — "...nor have they any control over death or life or resurrection" means they do not own death such that they could ward it off from their worshippers or whomever they wish, nor life such that they could take it from whomever they wish or bestow it upon whomever they wish, nor resurrection (نشورا) such that they could raise people to recompense them for their deeds. The ownership of these matters is among the requirements of divinity.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Ibn Sinan, from someone he mentioned, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the Quran and the Criterion (الفرقان)—are they two things or one thing? He said: "The Quran is the entirety of the Book, and the Criterion (الفرقان) is the clear (المحكم) part whose practice is obligatory."

In al-Ikhtisas by al-Mufid: In the tradition of Abdullah ibn Salam to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he said: "Inform me, did Allah reveal a Book to you?" He said: "Yes." He asked: "And which Book is it?" He said: "The Criterion (الفرقان)." He asked: "And why did your Lord name it the Criterion?" He said: "Because its verses and chapters are separate (متفرق); it was revealed in other than tablets—unlike the other scrolls, the Torah, the Gospel, and the Psalms, which were all revealed in their entirety in tablets and papers." He said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad."

I say: Each of the two narrations looks at one of the two previously mentioned meanings of the Criterion (الفرقان).

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ إِفْكٌ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ وَأَعَانَهُۥ عَلَيْهِ قَوْمٌ ءَاخَرُونَ ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءُو ظُلْمًا وَزُورًا (٤)
The faithless say, ‘This is nothing but a lie that he has fabricated, and other people have abetted him in it.’ Thus they have certainly come out with wrongdoing and falsehood. (4)
وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ ٱكْتَتَبَهَا فَهِىَ تُمْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا (٥)
They say, ‘He has taken down myths of the ancients, and they are dictated to him morning and evening.’ (5)
قُلْ أَنزَلَهُ ٱلَّذِى يَعْلَمُ ٱلسِّرَّ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٦)
Say, ‘It has been sent down by Him who knows the hidden in the heavens and the earth. Indeed He is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (6)
وَقَالُوا۟ مَالِ هَـٰذَا ٱلرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشِى فِى ٱلْأَسْوَاقِ ۙ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُۥ نَذِيرًا (٧)
And they say, ‘What sort of apostle is this who eats food and walks in the marketplaces? Why has not an angel been sent down to him so as to be a warner along with him?’ (7)
أَوْ يُلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ كَنزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُۥ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْهَا ۚ وَقَالَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَّسْحُورًا (٨)
Or, ‘[Why is not] a treasure thrown to him, or [why does] he [not] have a garden from which he may eat?’ And the wrongdoers say, ‘You are just following a bewitched man.’ (8)
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ فَضَلُّوا۟ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا (٩)
Look, how they coin epithets for you; so they go astray, and cannot find the way. (9)
تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِن شَآءَ جَعَلَ لَكَ خَيْرًا مِّن ذَٰلِكَ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ وَيَجْعَل لَّكَ قُصُورًۢا (١٠)
Blessed is He who will grant you better than that if He wishes—gardens with streams running in them, and He will make for you palaces. (10)
بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلسَّاعَةِ ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِمَن كَذَّبَ بِٱلسَّاعَةِ سَعِيرًا (١١)
Indeed, they deny the Hour, and We have prepared a Blaze for those who deny the Hour. (11)
إِذَا رَأَتْهُم مِّن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ سَمِعُوا۟ لَهَا تَغَيُّظًا وَزَفِيرًا (١٢)
When it sights them from a distant place, they will hear it raging and roaring. (12)
وَإِذَآ أُلْقُوا۟ مِنْهَا مَكَانًا ضَيِّقًا مُّقَرَّنِينَ دَعَوْا۟ هُنَالِكَ ثُبُورًا (١٣)
And when they are cast into a narrow place in it, bound together [in chains], they will pray for [their own] annihilation. (13)
لَّا تَدْعُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ثُبُورًا وَٰحِدًا وَٱدْعُوا۟ ثُبُورًا كَثِيرًا (١٤)
[They will be told:] ‘Do not pray for a single annihilation today, but pray for many annihilations!’ (14)
قُلْ أَذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ أَمْ جَنَّةُ ٱلْخُلْدِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۚ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَزَآءً وَمَصِيرًا (١٥)
Say, ‘Is that better, or the everlasting paradise promised to the Godwary, which will be their reward and destination?’ (15)
لَّهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَآءُونَ خَـٰلِدِينَ ۚ كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ وَعْدًا مَّسْـُٔولًا (١٦)
There they will have whatever they wish, abiding [forever]—a promise [much] besought, [binding] on your Lord. (16)
وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَقُولُ ءَأَنتُمْ أَضْلَلْتُمْ عِبَادِى هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَمْ هُمْ ضَلُّوا۟ ٱلسَّبِيلَ (١٧)
On the day that He will muster them and those whom they worship besides Allah, He will say, ‘Was it you who led astray these servants of Mine, or did they themselves stray from the way?’ (17)
قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ مَا كَانَ يَنۢبَغِى لَنَآ أَن نَّتَّخِذَ مِن دُونِكَ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ وَلَـٰكِن مَّتَّعْتَهُمْ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ نَسُوا۟ ٱلذِّكْرَ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بُورًا (١٨)
They will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove us to take any wali in Your stead! But You provided for them and their fathers until they forgot the Reminder, and they were a ruined lot.’ (18)
فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوكُم بِمَا تَقُولُونَ فَمَا تَسْتَطِيعُونَ صَرْفًا وَلَا نَصْرًا ۚ وَمَن يَظْلِم مِّنكُمْ نُذِقْهُ عَذَابًا كَبِيرًا (١٩)
So they will certainly impugn you in what you say, and you will neither be able to circumvent [punishment] nor find help, and whoever of you does wrong, We shall make him taste a terrible punishment. (19)
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّآ إِنَّهُمْ لَيَأْكُلُونَ ٱلطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشُونَ فِى ٱلْأَسْوَاقِ ۗ وَجَعَلْنَا بَعْضَكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ فِتْنَةً أَتَصْبِرُونَ ۗ وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ بَصِيرًا (٢٠)
We did not send any apostles before you but that they indeed ate food and walked in marketplaces. We have made you a [means of] test for one another, [to see] if you will be patient and steadfast, and your Lord is all-seeing. (20)

(Commentary)

The verses recount the criticisms leveled by the polytheists (المشركون) against the Noble Quran and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and provide the response to them.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ هَذا إِلَّا إِفْكٌ افْتَراهُ وَأَعانَهُ عَلَيْهِ قَوْمٌ آخَرُونَ» — "The faithless say, ‘This is nothing but a lie (إفك) that he has fabricated (افتراه), and other people have helped him at it’" [25:4]. The use of the expression "the faithless say" (قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) instead of simply "they say," despite the previous mention of the disbelievers in His saying, "And they have taken gods besides Him" (وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً), is an allusion (تلويح) that the speakers of these words are specifically the disbelievers of the Arabs, rather than polytheists in general.

The object referred to by their saying "This" (إِنْ هَذا) is the Noble Quran. They sufficed with a demonstrative pronoun rather than mentioning it by name or attribute out of contempt (إزراء) and to diminish its status.

Lie (الإفك) refers to speech that is turned away from its proper face (المصروف عن وجهه). Their meaning by it being a "fabricated lie" is that it is a falsehood (كذب) invented by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and attributed to Allah, the Glorified.

The context is not devoid of an intimation (إيماء) that the "other people" (قوم آخرون) refers to some of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). It has been mentioned in some reports that these people were ‘Addas, the client of Huwaytib ibn ‘Abd al-‘Uzza; Yasar, the client of al-‘Ala’ ibn al-Hadrami; and Jabr, the client of ‘Amir. They were from the People of the Book who read the Torah and had embraced Islam. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to visit them, and thus the claim was made.

His saying: «فَقَدْ جاؤُ ظُلْماً وَزُوراً» — "They have certainly come up with an injustice (ظلم) and a falsehood (زور)" [25:4]. It is stated in Majma‘ al-Bayan: "The verbs 'to come' (جاء) and 'to arrive' (أتى) are sometimes used in the sense of 'to do' (فعل), thus becoming transitive like it. So the meaning of the verse is: 'They have committed an injustice and a lie.'" It is also said that "injustice" (ظُلْماً) is in the accusative case due to the removal of a preposition, the implied meaning being "they have come with an injustice." Another view is that it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال), meaning "they have come as wrongdoers," but this is considered weak (سخيف).

It is also mentioned therein: "If it is asked: 'How was this amount sufficient as a response to them?' We say: 'Since the challenge (التحدي) had already been issued and they were unable to produce anything like it, it was sufficient here to merely point that out.'" End quote. It appears that the response to their saying, "This is nothing but a lie he has fabricated," and their saying, "Fables of the ancients (أساطير الأولين) which he has written down," is collectively His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ أَنْزَلَهُ الَّذِي يَعْلَمُ السِّرَّ» — "Say, ‘He sent it down who knows the secret...’" [25:6], as we shall explain. The sentence "They have certainly come up with an injustice and a falsehood" is an absolute rejection of their claim, functioning as a denial (المنع) supported by evidence (السند), and its evidence is the verses containing the challenge.

In summary, the meaning of the verse is: The disbelievers among the Arabs said, "This Quran is nothing but speech turned from its proper face—since it is the speech of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) which he has attributed to Allah—he has fabricated it against Allah, and other people, namely some of the People of the Book, have helped him with this speech." Thus, these disbelievers have committed an injustice and a lie by this statement of theirs.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقالُوا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ اكْتَتَبَها فَهِيَ تُمْلى عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلاً» — "And they say, ‘Fables of the ancients (أساطير الأولين) which he has written down (اكْتَتَبَها), and they are dictated (تُمْلى) to him morning and evening’" [25:5]. Fables (الأساطير) is the plural of usturah, meaning a written account, and it is predominantly used for mythical or superstitious tales (الأخبار الخرافية). Writing down (الاكتتاب) refers to the act of writing. Attributing it to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) despite his being unlettered (أمي) and unable to write is a form of metaphor (تجوز), in the sense that it was written at his request. Just as a prince says, "I wrote such-and-such to so-and-so," while it was actually his scribe who wrote it by his command. The evidence for this is His subsequent saying: "and they are dictated to him morning and evening," for if he were the one writing, there would be no meaning for "dictation" (الإملاء). It is also said that iktitab means istiktab (requesting someone to write).

Dictation (الإملاء) is the delivery of speech to a listener in its exact wording so that he may memorize and comprehend it, or to a scribe so that he may write it. The intended meaning in the verse is the first sense, as suggested by the context of "he has written them down, and they are dictated to him," for its outward meaning implies that the writing down occurred all at once, while the dictation occurs gradually and continuously. Thus, they are written and collected with him, read to him time after time, and he memorizes them and then recites to the people what he has comprehended and memorized.

Morning and evening (البكرة والأصيل) refer to the early morning and the late afternoon. It is an idiom (كناية) for "time after time." It is also said to mean the beginning of the day before people leave their homes and the end of the day after they return to them, which would be an idiom for them being dictated to him in secret (خفية).

The verse serves as an explanation for the preceding verse; it is as if they are clarifying their statement that "it is a lie he fabricated and others helped him with" by claiming that they wrote for him the fables of the ancients and then dictated them to him time after time, reading them bit by bit, while he recites them to the people and attributes them to Allah, the Glorified.

Thus, the entire verse is part of the speech of the disbelievers. It has been suggested that His saying, "he has written them down, and they are dictated to him..." until the end of the verse, is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, and not part of their speech, acting as a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) rebuking their claim of "fables of the ancients." However, the context does not support this.

His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ أَنْزَلَهُ الَّذِي يَعْلَمُ السِّرَّ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً» — "Say, ‘He sent it down who knows the secret (السر) in the heavens and the earth. Indeed, He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful’" [25:6]. This is a command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to refute their claim and their denial in which they accused the Quran of being a fabricated lie and fables of the ancients that he wrote down and are dictated to him time after time.

His being described as the one who "knows the secret"—meaning the hidden matters and the inner realities (بواطنها) in the heavens and the earth—is to signal (الإيذان) that this Book which He sent down contains secrets (أسرار) hidden from human intellects. It also contains an allusion (تعريض) to their punishment for their crimes, among which is their labeling the Quran as a fabricated lie and fables, all of which is known to Allah.

His saying: «إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً» — "Indeed, He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful" [25:6] is a justification (تعليل) for what is observed of His granting them respite (إمهال) and delaying their punishment for their crimes, their denial of the truth, and their audacity against Allah, the Glorified.

The meaning is: Say that the Quran is neither a fabricated lie nor fables as they claim; rather, it is a Book sent down from Allah, the Glorified, in which He has embedded hidden secrets that your intellects cannot fathom and your minds cannot encompass. Your labeling it as a lie and fables and your denial of its truths is a grave crime for which you deserve punishment, except that Allah, the Glorified, has granted you respite and delayed the punishment for your crime because He is characterized by forgiveness and mercy, which entails the delaying of punishment. This is a summary of what they [the commentators] have mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse.

However, the context does not support this. The gist of the verse's meaning according to their interpretation reduces to refuting the disbelievers' claim that the Quran is a fabricated lie and fables by claiming it is sent from Allah and contains hidden secrets they cannot access. In the context of a polemic (مخاصمة), it is not viable to refute one claim with another similar claim, or one that is even more obscure.

Furthermore, the justification with His saying, "Indeed, He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful," is only appropriate for the total negation of punishment, not for mere respite and delay. The names appropriate for respite and delay are those like the All-Forbearing (الحليم), the All-Knowing (العليم), and the All-Wise (الحكيم), rather than the All-Forgiving and All-Merciful.

What is more fitting for the context of polemic and defense through the manifestation of truth, and for the justification through forgiveness and mercy, is that His saying, "Indeed, He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful," be a justification for the sending down of the Book. It was mentioned previously that He sent it down to His servant to be a warner to all the worlds, and this is the prophethood (النبوة). In this case, His description as knowing the secret in the heavens and the earth would be an intimation that within their own secrets is that which necessitates the inclusion of divine forgiveness and mercy for their state—namely, their seeking, by their primordial nature (الفطرة) and innate disposition (جبلتهم), for happiness and a good end. The reality of this is nothing other than human happiness through the encompassment of forgiveness and mercy, even if many of them err in applying it to the enjoyment of the worldly life and its vanishing ornaments. Thus, it becomes a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) for the truth of the prophetic call contained in the Quran, and the falsehood of the claim that it is a lie from the fables of the ancients.

The formulation of the proof is that Allah, the Glorified, knows the secret in the heavens and the earth, and He knows that in your secret—established in your inner selves and upon which your primordial nature is fashioned—is a love for happiness and a quest for a good end, the reality of which is success in this world and the Hereafter. Since He, the Glorified, is All-Forgiving and All-Merciful, this necessitates that He respond to what you ask for in your secret and through the tongue of your primordial nature, by guiding you to His path which guarantees you happiness.

This is a Book that speaks to you of His path; it is not a lie fabricated against Allah, nor is it of the fables. Rather, it is a Book that contains what you ask for by your primordial nature and what you call for in your secret. If you respond to its caller, forgiveness and mercy will encompass you, and if you turn away, you will be deprived of that. Thus, it is a Book sent down from Allah. If it were not sent from Him as it claims, it would not guide to the reality of happiness, nor would it call to the pure truth. Its explanations would have differed, calling you at times to what contains your good and benefit—which is what brings you forgiveness and mercy—and at other times to what is evil and harmful for you—which is what provokes divine wrath and necessitates punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقالُوا ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ وَيَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْواقِ لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيراً أَوْ يُلْقى إِلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْها» — "And they say, ‘What is it with this apostle that he eats food and walks in the marketplaces? Why has not an angel been sent down to him so that he may be a warner along with him? Or why is not a treasure thrown to him, or why does he not have a garden from which he may eat?’" [25:7-8]. This is a narration of their criticisms against the Messenger after narrating their criticism of the Quran in His saying, "The faithless say, ‘This is nothing but a lie...’"

Their referring to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) as "this apostle" (هذَا الرَّسُولِ) while denying his message is based on sarcasm (تهكم) and mockery (استهزاء).

Their saying, "What is it with this apostle that he eats food and walks in the marketplaces?" is a question of wonder (تعجيب). The reason for this is that the idolaters (الوثنيون) believe that a human being is not permitted to connect with the Unseen (الغيب) while his existence is tied to matter, immersed in its darkness, and tainted by its impurities. Therefore, they seek mediation to the Godhead through the angels, worshipping them so they may intercede for them with Allah and bring them closer to Him. Thus, the angels are the ones near to Allah, connected to the Unseen, and designated for the message, if there were to be a message. A human being has no part in that.

From this, the meaning of their saying, "What is it with this apostle that he eats food and walks in the marketplaces?" becomes clear. The intended meaning is that the message (الرسالة) is incompatible with eating food and walking in the markets to earn a living, for it is a connection to the Unseen that does not coexist with material attachments. It is nothing but a function of the angels. Therefore, they said in more than one place, as narrated by Allah: "Had Allah wished, He would have sent down angels" [23:24], or similar statements.

From this, it also becomes clear that their saying, "Why has not an angel been sent down to him so that he may be a warner along with him?" is a concession (تنزل) by the polytheists in their proposal. That is: How can this claimant to the message be a messenger while he eats food and walks in the markets, when a messenger can only be an angel purified from these material traits? If we were to concede and accept his message while he is a human, then let an angel be sent down to him to be a warner with him, so that the warning and the delivery of the message may be connected to the Unseen through the mediation of the angel.

Similarly, their saying, "Or why is not a treasure thrown to him?" is a further concession from the previous proposal. That is: If an angel is not sent down to him and he remains independent in the message as a human, then let a treasure (كنز) be thrown to him from the sky so he may spend it on his material needs and not have to toil in the markets to earn his living. The descent of a treasure to him is easier than the descent of an angel to assist him in delivering the message.

Likewise, their saying, "Or why does he not have a garden from which he may eat?" is a further concession. The meaning is: If a treasure is not thrown to him, then let him have a garden (جنة) from which he may eat so he does not need to earn a living. This is easier than throwing a treasure to him.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقالَ الظَّالِمُونَ إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلاً مَسْحُورا» — "And the wrongdoers (الظالمون) say, ‘You are following none but a bewitched (مسحور) man’" [25:8]. The "wrongdoers" refers to the aforementioned proposers—as has been said. This is an instance of using a manifest noun in place of a pronoun, and describing them as "wrongdoers" is to indicate the extent of their injustice and audacity against Allah and His Messenger.

Their saying, "You are following none but..." is an address from them to the believers to reproach them and lead them away from the path of truth. Their meaning by "a bewitched man" is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); they mean he is under a spell cast by some sorcerers, such that he imagines he is a messenger to whom the angel of revelation comes with the message and the Book.

His saying, the Exalted: «انْظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلاً» — "Look how they strike parables (الأمثال) for you! So they have gone astray, and cannot find a way" [25:9]. Parables (الأمثال) here means likenesses or descriptions. It is sometimes said that mathal here means "description" (الوصف), as in His saying: "The description (مَثَلُ) of the Paradise promised to the God-conscious is that in it are rivers of water..." [48:15]. The gist is: Look how they have described you! They have gone so far astray regarding you that their guidance to the truth cannot be hoped for. Such as their saying that he eats food and walks in the markets, so he is not fit for the message because a messenger must be a person of the Unseen with no attachment to matter, or at the very least, not in need of ordinary means to obtain a living. And such as their saying: "He is a bewitched man."

His saying: «فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلاً» — "So they have gone astray, and cannot find a way" [25:9]. That is, it resulted from these parables they struck for you that they went astray with a wandering such that they cannot return to the path of truth, and no guidance can be hoped for them. For he who misses the path might miss it by a slight deviation such that he may be expected to tread it again, but he might also turn his back to it, such that the further he proceeds, the further he becomes from it. And he who calls the Book of Allah "fables" and describes His Messenger as "bewitched," and continues to increase in obstinacy, stubbornness, and mockery of the truth—how can his guidance be hoped for in such a state?

His saying, the Exalted: «تَبارَكَ الَّذِي إِنْ شاءَ جَعَلَ لَكَ خَيْراً مِنْ ذلِكَ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكَ قُصُوراً» — "Blessed is He who, if He wishes, will assign to you something better than that: gardens with streams running in them, and He will assign you palaces" [25:10]. The reference in "than that" (مِنْ ذلِكَ) is to what they proposed in their saying, "or why does he not have a garden from which he may eat," or to the sum of what they mentioned of the treasure and the garden.

Palaces (القصور) is the plural of qasr, which is a lofty, fortified building. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "palaces" (قُصُوراً) is to indicate greatness and magnificence.

The verse serves as a response to their criticism of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and their proposal that an angel be sent down to him, or a treasure be thrown to him, or that he have a garden. However, there is a transition (التفات) from the first person to the third person; He did not say, "Say: If my Lord wishes, He will assign to me such-and-such," but rather turned to His saying: "Blessed is He who, if He wishes, will assign to you..."

This contains an allusion that they do not deserve a response and are not fit to be addressed, for they are aware of the corruption of what they proposed to him. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only mentioned to them that he is a human like them to whom revelation is given; he did not claim to have a power of the Unseen or a divine sovereignty over everything he wants or is wanted from him. As He, the Exalted, said after narrating some of their proposals in Surah al-Isra’: "Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human, an apostle?’" [17:93].

So He, the Glorified, turned away from addressing them and from responding to what they proposed. He only mentioned to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that his Lord, who took him as a messenger and sent down to him the Criterion (الفرقان) — the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood — to be a warner to all the worlds, is capable of something greater than what they propose. If He wishes, He will assign to him something better than that: gardens with streams running in them, and He will assign him palaces whose description no describer can reach. That is better than having a garden from which he eats or a treasure being thrown to him to spend on his needs.

With this amount, their response regarding the treasure and the garden is achieved. As for the descent of an angel to him to share in the warning and assist him in delivery, no response was mentioned because its falsehood is manifest. Allah, the Exalted, has responded to it in various places of His speech with different answers, such as His saying: "Had We made him an angel, We would have surely made him a man, and We would have surely confused for them what they are confusing" [6:9], and His saying: "Say, ‘Had there been angels walking at peace on the earth, We would have surely sent down an angel to them from the sky as an apostle’" [17:95], and His saying: "We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they would not be given any respite" [15:8]. The explanation of the proof of each of these verses has already preceded within their respective commentaries.

From this, it becomes clear that the intended meaning of assigning gardens and palaces to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is assigning them in this world, as necessitated by the context of polemic and the refutation of their claim. For the gist of the context is: They propose to you such-and-such, intending to incapacitate and silence you, but your Lord is capable of something greater than that; if He wishes, He will assign to you something better than that: gardens with streams running in them, etc. These are necessarily in this world, otherwise the dispute would not be settled.

By this, the corruption of what was reported from some—that the intended meaning is the gardens and palaces of the Hereafter—becomes evident. Even more corrupt is the statement of others that the intended meaning is assigning gardens with streams running in them in this world and assigning palaces in the Hereafter. This was sometimes supported by the observation that the expression for the gardens is "if He wishes, He assigned" (جَعَلَ), which is a past tense form indicating certainty and is appropriate for this world, while for the palaces it is "He will assign" (يَجْعَلْ), which is a future form appropriate for the Hereafter. This is despite the fact that a verb occurring in the clause of a condition is stripped of time, and the difference in expression is a stylistic variation (تفنن) and a renewal of the form of speech. Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: «بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالسَّاعَةِ وَأَعْتَدْنا لِمَنْ كَذَّبَ بِالسَّاعَةِ سَعِيراً» — "Rather, they have denied the Hour (الساعة), and We have prepared a blaze (سعيراً) for those who deny the Hour" [25:11]. This is a digression (إضراب) from their criticism of him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and their objection to his eating food and walking in the markets, which implies denial. That is: They did not deny you and reject your prophethood because you eat food and walk in the markets; that is merely formal speech from them. Rather, the original reason for their denial of your prophethood and their criticism of you is that they have denied the Hour and rejected the Resurrection (المعاد). It is well known that prophethood has no weight with the denial of the Hour, and there is no meaning for religion and law (الشريعة) if there were no reckoning and recompense.

The mention of the original reason after mentioning the objection, the proposal, and the response here is similar to what occurred in Surah al-Isra’ after mentioning the proposals and then the response, by mentioning the original reason in His saying: "Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human, an apostle?’ And nothing prevented the people from believing when guidance came to them except that they said, ‘Has Allah sent a human as an apostle?’" [17:93-94].

A group of commentators mentioned that His saying, "Rather, they have denied the Hour," is a narration of another of their falsehoods, just as He narrated others related to monotheism (التوحيد), the Book, and the message in His saying, "And they have taken gods besides Him," and His saying, "The faithless say, ‘This is nothing but a lie...’" and His saying, "And they say, ‘What is it with this apostle that he eats...’"

Then they branched out regarding the point of the digression (الإضراب). Some mentioned that the reason is that the Resurrection is beyond doubt; others said that its denial is greater; others said it is more wondrous, and so on.

The truth is that the context does not support this. The context dealing with their criticism of the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the response to it has not yet concluded, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: "We did not send any apostles before you but that they indeed ate food and walked in the marketplaces..." [25:20], and the verses following it. Thus, there is no meaning for the interruption of the narration of their denial of the Hour between the verses narrating their denial of the Messenger and responding to it. This is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَأَعْتَدْنا لِمَنْ كَذَّبَ بِالسَّاعَةِ سَعِيراً» — "and We have prepared a blaze (سعيراً) for those who deny the Hour" [25:11]. The relative pronoun and its clause are placed in the position of a returning pronoun to indicate that the recompense with the blaze is established for everyone who denies the Hour, they and others being equal in this, and that the reason for preparing the blaze for them is their denial of the Hour.

The word "the Hour" is placed a second time in the position of its pronoun to be more explicit and clear, which is appropriate for the context of threat (التهديد). The blaze (السعير) is the ignited, flaming fire.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِذا رَأَتْهُمْ مِنْ مَكانٍ بَعِيدٍ سَمِعُوا لَها تَغَيُّظاً وَزَفِيراً» — "When it sees them from a far-off place, they will hear its raging (تغَيُّظاً) and sighing (زفيراً)" [25:12]. In al-Mufradat: "Rage (الغيظ) is the most intense anger... and raging (التغيظ) is the manifestation of rage, and it may be accompanied by an audible sound, as He said: 'they will hear its raging and sighing.'" End quote. Also therein: "Sighing (الزفير) is the repetition of breath until the ribs swell from it." End quote.

The verse depicts the state of the Fire in relation to them when they appear before it on the Day of Recompense—that it intensifies when they appear to it, like a lion that roars when it sees its prey.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَإِذا أُلْقُوا مِنْها مَكاناً ضَيِّقاً مُقَرَّنِينَ دَعَوْا هُنالِكَ ثُبُوراً» — "And when they are cast into a narrow place in it, tied together (مقرنين), they will plead there for death (ثبوراً)" [25:13]. "Place" (مَكاناً) is in the accusative case by the implication of "in." Death (الثبور) refers to woe and destruction.

Tied together (التقرين) means being bound in shackles and chains. It is also said to mean being placed with their companions from the devils, but this is far from the literal meaning. The meaning is: And when they are cast on the Day of Recompense into a narrow place in the Fire while they are bound in shackles, they will plead there for an indescribable destruction, which is their saying: "O our destruction!"

His saying, the Exalted: «لا تَدْعُوا الْيَوْمَ ثُبُوراً واحِداً وَادْعُوا ثُبُوراً كَثِيراً» — "‘Do not plead for a single death today, but plead for many deaths!’" [25:14]. Appealing for woe and destruction is a type of ruse to escape from hardship. Since today is the Day of Recompense only, in which no deed benefits and no cause avails at all, their pleading for destruction will not benefit them in the least. Therefore, He, the Exalted, said: "Do not plead today..." etc. This is an idiom for the fact that destruction will not benefit you today, whether you ask for little of it or much. It is similar in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: "Enter it! Then be patient or impatient; it is the same for you" [52:16], and His saying narrating from them: "It is the same to us whether we are restless or patient; there is no escape for us" [14:21].

It is said the meaning is that your punishment is long, eternal, and not interrupted by a single destruction, but rather requires many destructions. This is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ أَذلِكَ خَيْرٌ أَمْ جَنَّةُ الْخُلْدِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ — until His saying — مَسْؤُلاً» — "Say, ‘Is that better, or the Garden of Abiding (جنة الخلد) which has been promised to the God-conscious (المتقون)?...’" [25:15-16]. The reference in "that" (أَذلِكَ) is to the blaze with its described attributes. He commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to ask them which of the two is more preferable: the blaze or the Garden of Abiding? The question is about a self-evident matter (أمر بديهي) in which no rational person would hesitate. This is a common technique in debate and polemic, where the opponent is given a choice between two things, one of which is self-evidently correct and the other self-evidently false; he is then tasked to choose one. If he chooses the truth, he has admitted what he used to deny, and if he chooses the falsehood, he is disgraced.

His saying: «أَمْ جَنَّةُ الْخُلْدِ» — "or the Garden of Abiding." The annexation of the Garden to "Abiding" (الخلد)—which is permanence—is to indicate that it is in itself eternal and does not perish, just as His subsequent saying: "abiding" (خالِدِينَ) is to indicate that its inhabitants are eternal within it and there is no way for perishing to reach them.

His saying: «وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ» — "promised to the God-conscious." Its implied structure is "the God-conscious were promised it," because the verb "to promise" (وعد) takes two objects, and "the God-conscious" is the second object acting as the subject of the passive verb.

His saying: «كانَتْ لَهُمْ جَزاءً وَمَصِيراً» — "It shall be their reward (جزاء) and destination (مصيراً)" [25:15]. That is, a reward for their godwariness and a place of return to which they return by virtue of being God-conscious. As He, the Exalted, said: "Indeed, the God-conscious shall be amid gardens and springs... and they shall not be expelled from them" [15:45-48]. This is among the decrees He decreed on the day He created Adam and commanded the angels and Iblis to prostrate to him, by which the reward and destination of the God-conscious are determined, as previously explained in the commentary of Surah al-Hijr.

His saying: «لَهُمْ فِيها ما يَشاؤُنَ خالِدِينَ» — "They will have in it whatever they wish, abiding [there forever]" [25:16]. That is, they possess within it, by Allah's granting them possession, everything to which their will (مشيتهم) attaches. Their will only attaches to what they love and desire, unlike the people of the Fire, as He, the Exalted, said regarding them: "And a barrier will be set between them and what they desire" [34:54]. They do not love or desire except that which is truly worthy of love, which is what Allah loves for them—namely, the good and happiness they deserve, by which they are perfected and by which neither they nor others are harmed. Understand this.

By this explanation, it becomes clear that they have an absolute will; they are given whatever they wish and intend, except that they do not wish except what contains the pleasure of their Lord. This resolves the difficulty raised regarding the verses speaking of absolute will—like this verse—that the necessity of absolute will is that it should be permissible for them to intend some sins, foul acts, atrocities, and vanity, or to intend something that grieves other inhabitants of Paradise, or to intend the salvation of some of those eternally in the Fire, or to intend the stations of the prophets and the sincere among the saints who are of a higher degree than them, and so on.

How could that be? When He, the Exalted, has said: "O soul at peace! Return to your Lord, pleased, pleasing! Then enter among My servants! And enter My paradise!" [89:27-30]. So they are pleased with what Allah is pleased with, and they are pleasing; they do not intend except what He approves. Thus, they do not intend a sin, a foul act, an atrocity, vanity, or a lie. They do not intend what their fellow inhabitants of Paradise do not approve of. They do not intend the removal of punishment from those whom their Lord intends to punish. They do not wish for or desire the station of one who is of a higher degree than them, because the one who specifically assigned it to them is their Lord, and they have become pleased with what He has done and loved what He has loved.

His saying, the Exalted: «كانَ عَلى رَبِّكَ وَعْداً مَسْؤُلاً» — "It is a promise (وعداً) binding upon your Lord, [and] to be pleaded for (مسؤولاً)" [25:16]. That is, this promise which He promised the God-conscious was a promise binding upon your Lord, which He must fulfill. He, the Exalted, only made it binding upon Himself when He decreed that on the first day, and informed of it with statements like His saying: "Indeed, for the God-conscious there is a good destination: the Gardens of Eden... This is what you are promised for the Day of Reckoning" [38:49-53].

The reason for this promise being described as "to be pleaded for" (مسؤولاً) is that the God-conscious asked their Lord for it through the tongue of their state and their readiness, or they asked Him for it in their supplications, or the angels asked for it as Allah narrates from them: "Our Lord, admit them into the Gardens of Eden..." etc. [40:8], or all of these requests combined.

Al-Tabarsi (may Allah have mercy on him) mentioned regarding the verse that His saying, "It shall be their reward and destination," is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun of the Garden implied in "promised to the God-conscious," and that His saying, "They will have in it whatever they wish," is a circumstantial qualifier for "the God-conscious." This is closer to the mind than the statement of others that the two sentences are new beginnings (استينافان) in the position of justification, like a response to an implied question.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ وَما يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ» — "On the day He will muster them and what they worship besides Allah..." [25:17] until the end of the verse. The four plural pronouns refer to the disbelievers. The meaning of "what they worship" refers to the angels, the worshipped among humans, and the idols—if "what" (ما) is general for non-rational beings; otherwise, it refers to the idols only.

Those referred to and intended by His saying, "these servants of Mine" (عِبادِي هؤُلاءِ), are the disbelievers. The meaning of the verse is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالُوا سُبْحانَكَ ما كانَ يَنْبَغِي لَنا أَنْ نَتَّخِذَ مِنْ دُونِكَ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ» — "They will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It did not behoove us to take any guardians (أولياء) besides You...’" [25:18] until the end. This is the response of the worshipped ones to His saying, "Was it you who led these servants of Mine astray?..." etc. They began with glorification (التسبيح) as is the etiquette of servitude (أدب العبودية) in instances where the polytheism of the polytheists is mentioned, or what might suggest it in some way.

His saying: "It did not behoove us to take any guardians besides You" means: It was not right or proper for us to bypass You for another and take guardians besides You—and they are the ones who worshipped us and took us as guardians besides You. His saying: «وَلكِنْ مَتَّعْتَهُمْ وَآباءَهُمْ حَتَّى نَسُوا الذِّكْرَ وَكانُوا قَوْماً بُوراً» — "But You gave them and their fathers enjoyment until they forgot the Remembrance (الذكر) and they were a lost (بوراً) people" [25:18]. Lost (البور) is the plural of ba’ir, meaning one who perishes; it is also said to mean the corrupt.

When the questioned worshipped ones negated the attribution of leading their worshippers astray from themselves, they began to attribute it to the disbelievers themselves, while explaining the cause that led them astray—namely, that they were a perishing or corrupt people. You gave them and their fathers enjoyment of the goods and blessings of the worldly life until the enjoyment lasted long for them as a trial and test. So they enjoyed them and became occupied with them until they forgot the Remembrance brought by the messengers, and thus they turned away from monotheism to polytheism.

So, their being a perishing or corrupt people due to their devotion to the world and their immersion in desires is the cause of their absorption in enjoyment and the diversion of their concerns to preoccupation with means. This is the cause for their forgetting the Remembrance and turning away from monotheism to polytheism.

Thus, it becomes clear that His saying, "and they were a lost people," is part of the completion of the response. As for those who made the sentence a parenthetical concluding remark (اعتراض تذييلي) confirming the content of what preceded it, and inferred from it that the original cause of their straying is that they were, in their essences, wretched and perishing—and that this is only by a decisive decree from Him, the Exalted, in His prior knowledge, so He is the one who truly led them astray, and it was only attributed to themselves out of etiquette—this is problematic.

Firstly: It corrupts the meaning of the verse, for there would be no reason then to introduce the rectification (الاستدراك) with His saying, "But You gave them and their fathers enjoyment until they forgot the Remembrance," as it would be a superfluous addition with no need for it.

Secondly: Attributing perdition (البوار) and wretchedness (الشقاء) to the essences of things contradicts what rational beings have agreed upon by their primordial nature regarding the effect of teaching and upbringing. Sense and experience support this, and it contradicts both the view of free will (الاختيار) and compulsion (الجبر). As for the contradiction with free will, it is manifest. As for the contradiction with compulsion, it is because the proponent of compulsion restricts causality to the Necessary Being, the Exalted, and negates it from others, which is contradicted by attributing necessary requirement to the essences and quiddities (ماهياتها) of things.

Thirdly: It involves a confusion in the meaning of the decree (القضاء) regarding its object. The fact that the decree is decisive does not necessitate the exit of the act to which it is attached from free will into compulsion. For the decree only attaches to the act with its limits, which is its proceeding from the choice of the agent insofar as it proceeds from his choice. Thus, its attachment necessitates the emphasis of its being volitional, not that it removes the attribute of choice from it.

Fourthly: Their statement that "the one who leads astray in reality is Allah, and they only attributed the straying to the disbelievers themselves out of etiquette"—and they similarly stated regarding the attribution of sins, foul deeds, atrocities, and horrific tragedies to their doers, that while they are among His acts, the Exalted, they are only attributed to others out of etiquette—is a self-contradictory statement. Etiquette (الأدب)—as detailed in the sixth volume of this book—is the beautiful form in which an act should occur; in other words, the elegance of the act. Since the explicit truth regarding an unbeautiful act is that it is the act of Allah, the Glorified, and no one else shares in His act in any way assumed, then its attribution to other than Him, the Exalted, would be a false attribution, not true, and a lie and fabrication that does not correspond to reality. I wonder, what beautiful etiquette is there in abolishing an explicit truth and reviving a falsehood? And what elegance and grace is there in lying and fabrication by ascribing an act to other than its doer?

Allah, the Glorified, is too exalted to be magnified by falsehood, or by covering up some of His acts, or by lying and fabrication by ascribing some of what He does to others. Since He is beautiful and does nothing but the beautiful, what is the meaning of "etiquette" by negating some of His acts from Him?

His saying, the Exalted: «فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوكُمْ بِما تَقُولُونَ فَما تَسْتَطِيعُونَ صَرْفاً وَلا نَصْراً» — "‘They have already denied you in what you say; so you can neither avert [the punishment] nor [find any] help’" [25:19] until the end of the verse. This is speech from Him, the Exalted, which He delivers to the polytheists after the worshipped ones' disavowal of them. As for the speech of the worshipped ones, it concluded with His saying, "and they were a lost people."

The meaning is: The worshipped ones have already denied you in what you say regarding them—that they are gods besides Allah who avert evil from their worshippers and help them. Since they have denied you and negated divinity and guardianship from themselves, you, O worshippers, cannot avert the punishment from yourselves due to your worship of them, nor can you find any help for yourselves through them.

The choice between "averting" (الصرف) and "help" (النصر) is perhaps based on the worshipped ones' independence in repelling the punishment from them—which is averting—and their lack of independence such that they would be part of the cause—which is help.

Others besides ‘Asim, through the path of Hafs, read "they cannot" (يستطيعون) with the letter ya’. This is a good reading that accords with the requirement of the context. The meaning would be: The worshipped ones have already denied you in what you say—that they are gods who avert evil from you or help you—and it follows from that that they cannot provide for you any averting or help.

His saying: «وَمَنْ يَظْلِمْ مِنْكُمْ نُذِقْهُ عَذاباً كَبِيراً» — "‘And whoever among you does wrong (يظلم), We shall make him taste a great punishment’" [25:19]. The intended meaning of "wrong" (الظلم) is absolute wrongdoing and disobedience, even if the subject of the preceding verses was specifically the wrongdoing which is polytheism. So His saying, "And whoever among you does wrong..." etc., is an instance of placing a general law in the position of a specific ruling. If the intended meaning were the specific ruling for them, the speech would have properly been: "and We shall make you taste a great punishment because of your wrongdoing," for they are all wrongdoers with the wrongdoing of polytheism.

The point in this is to indicate that the divine ruling is effective and ongoing, with no preventer or reverser. It is as if it were said: Even if the worshipped ones denied you and were unable to provide averting or help, the general divine ruling—"whoever among you does wrong, We shall make him taste a great punishment"—in its effectiveness and continuity, has no preventer or reverser, so you are certainly tasters of the punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَما أَرْسَلْنا قَبْلَكَ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا إِنَّهُمْ لَيَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعامَ وَيَمْشُونَ فِي الْأَسْواقِ» — "We did not send any apostles before you but that they indeed ate food and walked in the marketplaces..." [25:20] until the end of the verse. He, the Exalted, responded to their saying, "What is it with this apostle that he eats food and walks in the marketplaces?..." etc., first with His saying, "Blessed is He who, if He wishes, will assign to you something better than that..." etc., along with what follows it of His saying, "Rather, they have denied the Hour..." etc. This is a second response, the gist of which is that this Messenger is not the first messenger sent to the people; rather, Allah sent before him a vast multitude of messengers who followed the human custom current among people, eating food and walking in the markets. He did not create for them a garden from which they eat, nor was a treasure thrown to them, nor was an angel sent down with them. This Messenger is but like one of them and has not come with an unprecedented matter (أمر بددع) such that something should be expected from him that is not expected from others.

Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying: "Say, ‘I am not a novelty (بدعاً) among the apostles, nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you. I just follow what is revealed to me’" [46:9], and close in meaning to His saying: "Say, ‘I am only a human like you, [into whom] it is revealed...’" [18:110].

If it is said: This is in reality a defense of the objection against him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) specifically, while directing it toward the generality of the messengers. So they may object to the generality of the messengers just as their predecessors directed it. Allah has narrated that from them, saying: "They said, ‘Shall humans guide us?’" [64:6], and He said: "They said, ‘You are nothing but humans like us’" [14:10], and He said: "‘This is nothing but a human like you, who eats of what you eat and drinks of what you drink’" [23:33].

We say: The response corresponds to the objection. For their saying, "What is it with this apostle that he eats..." etc., clearly implies specificity. As for the generalization of the objection, if it were generalized, it is refuted by His saying, the Exalted: "Rather, they have denied the Hour..." etc., and His saying before that: "Say, ‘He sent it down who knows the secret...’" etc., according to the previously mentioned explanation.

Among the strange statements is what is reported from some commentators that the verse is a consolation (تسلية) for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as if it were said: The messengers before you were in the same state as you, so you have in them a good example. As for it being a response to their obstinacy, the structure does not support it, as it was already responded to by His saying: "Look how they strike parables for you!" This is an error.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَجَعَلْنا بَعْضَكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ فِتْنَةً أَتَصْبِرُونَ» — "‘We have made some of you a test (فتنة) for others: will you be patient (أَتَصْبِرُونَ)?’" [25:20]. This completes the previous response, acting as a justification for the messengers being like other people in human characteristics, without their lives or their call being distinguished by celestial characteristics that would produce certainty of their carrying the divine message—such as the descent of an angel to them, the throwing of a treasure to them, or the creation of a garden for them. It is as if it were said: The reason for the messengers conducting their lives as people do is that We have made some people a test for others by which they are tried. So the messengers are a test for other people; they are tried by them, so that the people of doubt may be distinguished from the people of faith, and the followers of desires—who are not patient with the bitterness of truth—from the seekers of truth who are patient in the obedience of Allah and the treading of His path.

From what has passed, it becomes clear: Firstly: That the intended meaning of patience (الصبر) is patience in all its types: patience in the obedience of Allah, patience against His disobedience, and patience during calamities. Secondly: That His saying, "We have made some of you a test for others," is an instance of placing a general ruling in the position of a specific one. What is required is to point to the making of the messengers—in this state of theirs—a test for other people.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَكانَ رَبُّكَ بَصِيراً» — "And your Lord is All-Seeing (بصيراً)" [25:20]. That is, He is knowledgeable of what is correct in matters, so He places every matter in its appropriate position and thereby conducts the most perfect system. The goal of the human system is the perfection of every individual by his traversing the path of happiness or wretchedness according to what he is prepared for and deserves. This necessitates the spreading of the system of trial (الامتحان) among them, and this necessitates the removal of distinction between the messengers and others.

In the sentence, there is a transition (التفات) from the first person plural to the third person. The point in it is similar to what was in His previous saying: "Blessed is He who, if He wishes..." etc.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas: That ‘Utbah and Shaybah, the sons of Rabi‘ah; Abu Sufyan ibn Harb; al-Nadr ibn al-Harith; Abu al-Bakhtari; al-Aswad ibn al-Muttalib; Zam‘ah ibn al-Aswad; al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah; Abu Jahl ibn Hisham; ‘Abdullah ibn Umayyah; Umayyah ibn Khalaf; al-‘As ibn Wa’il; and Nabih ibn al-Hajjaj gathered together. Some of them said to others: "Send for Muhammad, speak to him, and argue with him so that you may be excused regarding him." So they sent to him saying: "The nobles of your people have gathered for you to speak to you."

He said: So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came to them, and they said to him: "O Muhammad, we have sent for you to be excused regarding you. If you have only brought this discourse seeking wealth, we will collect for you from our wealth. If you seek honor, we will make you our chief. If you seek kingship, we will make you our king."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I have nothing of what you say. I did not come to you with what I brought seeking your wealth, nor honor among you, nor kingship over you. But Allah has sent me to you as a messenger, sent down a Book to me, and commanded me to be a bearer of good news and a warner to you. So I have delivered to you the message of my Lord and advised you. If you accept from me what I have brought you, it is your portion in this world and the Hereafter. And if you reject it, I will be patient for the command of Allah until Allah judges between me and you."

They said: "O Muhammad, if you are not accepting anything we have offered you, then ask for yourself—and ask your Lord to send an angel with you to confirm what you say and to argue with us on your behalf. And ask Him to assign for you gardens and palaces of gold and silver to enrich you from what you seek, for you stand in the markets and seek a living as we seek it, so that we may know your merit and your station with your Lord, if you are a messenger as you claim."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to them: "I am not one to do that. I am not one who asks his Lord for this, and I was not sent to you for this. But Allah has sent me as a bearer of good news and a warner."

So Allah sent down regarding their statement: «وَقالُوا ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ — until His saying — وَجَعَلْنا بَعْضَكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ فِتْنَةً. أَتَصْبِرُونَ وَكانَ رَبُّكَ بَصِيراً » — "And they say, ‘What is it with this apostle that he eats food...’" until "‘We have made some of you a test for others: will you be patient? And your Lord is All-Seeing’" [25:7-20]. That is: I have made some of you a trial for others so that you may be patient. Had I wished to place the world with My Messenger so that you would not oppose him, I would have done so.

In the same source, al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh narrated through the path of Mak-hul from Abu Umamah, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Whoever lies against me intentionally, let him take his seat between the two eyes of Hell." They said: "O Messenger of Allah, does Hell have eyes?" He said: "Have you not heard Allah saying: ‘When it sees them from a far-off place’? Does it see them except with two eyes?"

I say: It was also narrated from a man among the companions, and there is some obscurity in the evidence of the report.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Yahya ibn Abi Usayd: That the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was asked about the saying of Allah: «وَإِذا أُلْقُوا مِنْها مَكاناً ضَيِّقاً مُقَرَّنِينَ» — "And when they are cast into a narrow place in it, tied together..." [25:13]. He said: "By the One in whose hand is my soul, they are forced into the Fire just as a peg is forced into a wall."

Ayahs

۞ وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَىٰ رَبَّنَا ۗ لَقَدِ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْ عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا (٢١)
Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why have angels not been sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?’ Certainly, they are full of arrogance within their souls and have become terribly defiant. (21)
يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا (٢٢)
The day they will see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day, and they will say, ‘Keep off [from paradise]!’ (22)
وَقَدِمْنَآ إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هَبَآءً مَّنثُورًا (٢٣)
Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then turn them into scattered dust. (23)
أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُّسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا (٢٤)
On that day the inhabitants of paradise will be in the best abode and an excellent resting place. (24)
وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ بِٱلْغَمَـٰمِ وَنُزِّلَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ تَنزِيلًا (٢٥)
The day when the sky with its clouds will be split open, and the angels will be sent down [in a majestic] descent, (25)
ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۚ وَكَانَ يَوْمًا عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَسِيرًا (٢٦)
on that day true sovereignty will belong to the All-beneficent, and it will be a hard day for the faithless. (26)
وَيَوْمَ يَعَضُّ ٱلظَّالِمُ عَلَىٰ يَدَيْهِ يَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى ٱتَّخَذْتُ مَعَ ٱلرَّسُولِ سَبِيلًا (٢٧)
It will be a day when the wrongdoer will bite his hands, saying, ‘I wish I had followed the Apostle’s way! (27)
يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰ لَيْتَنِى لَمْ أَتَّخِذْ فُلَانًا خَلِيلًا (٢٨)
Woe to me! I wish I had not taken so and so as a friend! (28)
لَّقَدْ أَضَلَّنِى عَنِ ٱلذِّكْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ جَآءَنِى ۗ وَكَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ خَذُولًا (٢٩)
Certainly he led me astray from the Reminder after it had come to me, and Satan is a deserter of man.’ (29)
وَقَالَ ٱلرَّسُولُ يَـٰرَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِى ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ مَهْجُورًا (٣٠)
