And the Apostle will say, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people consigned this Quran to oblivion.’ (30)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا لِكُلِّ نَبِىٍّ عَدُوًّا مِّنَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ هَادِيًا وَنَصِيرًا (٣١)
That is how for every prophet We assigned an enemy from among the guilty, and your Lord suffices as helper and guide. (31)

(Commentary)

The verses recount another objection from the polytheists (المشركين) against the Messenger’s mission, by which they seek to refute him. The gist of it is that if it were permissible for a human being, qua human, to be a messenger upon whom angels descend with revelation (الوحي) from Allah, the Glorified, or who sees Him, the Exalted, such that He speaks to him through revelation, then the Messenger and all other humans would be equal in this characteristic. Thus, if his claim to messengership were true, we—or at least some of us—would see what he claims to see and experience within ourselves what he experiences.

This objection is among those preceded by the nations of past prophets, as Allah recounted: قَالُوا إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنَا — "They said, ‘You are nothing but humans like us’" (Ibrahim: 10). The explanation of this argument has been mentioned repeatedly.

This, along with their previous objection in their saying: مَا لِهَذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعَامَ — "What is with this apostle that he eats food..." (Al-Furqan: 7), constitutes a single argument that corners the opponent into one of two problems. The summary of its formulation is: if the messengership claimed by this Messenger is a heavenly gift and a connection to the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — in which a human, qua human, has no share, then let an angel descend to him to be a warner with him, or let a treasure be cast to him, or let there be a garden for him from which he eats. But if it is a characteristic within the capacity of a human, qua human, to attain and be described by, then why do we not find it within ourselves? "Why were the angels not sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?"

Allah, the Glorified, answered the first part with what has already been established, and answered the second by stating that they will indeed see the angels, but in a realm (نشأة) other than this worldly realm. The answer carries the meaning of His saying: مَا نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَمَا كَانُوا إِذًا مُنْظَرِينَ — "We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they will not be granted any respite" (Al-Hijr: 8), the explanation of which will follow. In these verses, there is an allusion to what follows death and the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا لَوْلَا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَى رَبَّنَا لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا — "Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why were the angels not sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?’ Certainly they are too proud of themselves and have become terribly defiant" (Al-Furqan: 21). It is stated in Majma‘ al-Bayan: "Expectation (الرجاء) is the anticipation of a good whose occurrence becomes strong in the soul; similar to it are craving (الطمع) and hope (الأمل). Encounter (اللقاء) is the reaching of a thing without any barrier. Defiance (العتو) is the departure toward the most heinous of injustices." End quote.

What is meant by "encounter" (اللقاء) is the return to Allah on the Day of Resurrection. It is so named because of their emergence before Him, the Exalted, such that no barrier of ignorance or heedlessness remains, due to the manifestation of Divine Grandeur, as He, the Exalted, said: وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ — "...and they will know that Allah is the Manifest Truth" (An-Nur: 25).

Thus, the meaning of their not "expecting the encounter" is their denial of the Return (المعاد) and their rejection of the Hour (الساعة). It was not expressed as "rejecting the Hour" or similar terms—as was done in previous verses—because of their mention of witnessing the angels and seeing the Lord, the Exalted and Sanctified. This contains an allusion that they only said what they said and requested the descent of angels or the vision of the Lord due to their despair of the encounter and their assumption of its impossibility; thus, they demanded what is impossible according to their own assumption.

Their saying: لَوْلَا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَى رَبَّنَا — "Why were the angels not sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?" is an objection from them against the Messenger’s mission, presented in the form of an exhortation, like their saying elsewhere: لَوْ مَا تَأْتِينَا بِالْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ — "Why do you not bring us the angels, if you are one of the truthful?" (Al-Hijr: 7). The formulation of the argument, as previously alluded to, is that if messengership—which is the descent of angels with revelation or the Exalted speaking to humans directly—were something accessible for humans to attain, and we are humans like this claimant of messengership, then why do the angels not descend upon us and why do we not see our Lord? Why then were the angels not sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?

What we have mentioned regarding the formulation is supported by the absolute mention of the descent of angels and the vision of the Lord without them saying: "Why were the angels not sent down to us so they may testify to your truth" or "or why do we not see our Lord so He may testify to your truth." Furthermore, they had mentioned in their previous objection the descent of an angel "to be a warner with him," which implies his confirmation.

In the expression of Him, the Exalted, with the term "our Lord" (ربنا), there is a type of sarcasm from them. For the polytheists did not view Him, the Exalted, as their Lord; rather, they held that their lords were those whom they worshipped, while Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord of lords. It is as if they said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "You claim that Allah is your Lord and that He has shown affection toward you, singling you out for direct address and speech, and that He is our Lord; so let Him show affection toward us and address us through vision as He did with you."

Moreover, they only turned away from worshipping the lords of the idols—who are the angels, the spiritualities of the planets, and the like—to the worship of idols and statues so that they would be perceptible and not absent from sight during worship and the offering of sacrifices.

And His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا — "Certainly they are too proud of themselves and have become terribly defiant" (Al-Furqan: 21). That is: I swear, they sought greatness for themselves without right and transgressed with a mighty transgression.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلَائِكَةَ لَا بُشْرَى يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَحْجُورًا — "The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day, and they will say, ‘Keep off!’" (Al-Furqan: 22). In al-Mufradat: "The hijr (الحجر) is that which is prohibited through its consecration. He, the Exalted, said: وَقَالُوا هَذِهِ أَنْعَامٌ وَحَرْثٌ حِجْرٌ — 'And they say, "These cattle and tillage are off limits"' (Al-An'am: 138). And: وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَحْجُورًا — 'and they will say, "Keep off!"' A man, when meeting someone he feared, would say that. Thus, the Exalted mentioned that the disbelievers (الكفار), when they see the angels, will say that, thinking it will benefit them." End quote.

It is narrated from al-Khalil: "A man in the Pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية) would see a man from whom he feared being killed during the sacred months, and he would say: hijran mahjuran (حجرًا محجورًا), meaning: 'It is forbidden for you to accost me in this month,' so no one would initiate harm against him." From Abu Ubaydah: "It is a formula of seeking refuge used by the Arabs; one who fears another in the Sanctuary (الحرم) or in a sacred month says it when they meet if there is a blood-feud between them."

Thus, His saying: يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلَائِكَةَ لَا بُشْرَى يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ — "The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day." "The day" (يوم), as has been said, is an adverbial qualifier (ظرف) for His saying: "no good news" (لا بشرى), and His saying: "on that day" (يومئذ) is an emphasis for it. What is meant by "no good news" (لا بشرى) is a categorical negation (نفي للجنس). What is meant by "the guilty" (المجرمين) is everyone characterized by guilt, though the specific context here is the guilt of polytheism (الشرك), and the guilty are those who do not expect the encounter, who were mentioned previously. The meaning is: the day these people who do not expect Our encounter see the angels, there shall be no good news—by way of categorical negation—on that day for the guilty, and they are among them.

And His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَحْجُورًا — "and they will say, ‘Keep off!’" The subject of "they say" (يقولون) is the polytheists. That is, the polytheists will say on that day to the angels, who are approaching them with punishment: "A forbidden sanctuary (حجرًا محجورًا)," meaning: "Let us be in a refuge from you." It is also said that the plural pronoun refers to the angels, and the meaning is: the angels say to the polytheists: "Forbidden and prohibited (حرامًا محرمًا) is it for you to hear good news," or "Forbidden and prohibited is it for you to enter Paradise," or "Forbidden and prohibited is it for you to seek refuge from the punishment in anything, for there is no refuge for you." This first meaning is closer to the context.

The verse serves as the answer to their saying: "Why were the angels not sent down to us?" It turns away from answering their saying: "or why do we not see our Lord?" for the vision they intended by their words was visual perception, which necessitates corporeality (التجسم) and materiality (المادية)—exalted is He far above that. As for vision with the eye of certainty (عين اليقين), which is the vision of the heart (الرؤية القلبية), they were not among those who could comprehend it, and even if they could, it was not what they intended.

As for the clarification of the answer regarding the matter of the angels' descent and their vision, the principle of vision has been taken as a given, accepted fact—that there is a day in which they will see the angels. However, the report concerning their state on the day of vision was placed in the position of reporting the principle of their vision to indicate that their request to see the angels does not result in their benefit. For they shall not see the angels except on the day they face the punishment of the Fire, and that is after the transition from the worldly realm to the other realm, as He alluded to elsewhere by His saying: مَا نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَمَا كَانُوا إِذًا مُنْظَرِينَ — "We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they will not be granted any respite" (Al-Hijr: 8). Thus, in this request of theirs, they are seeking to hasten the punishment, while they think they are frustrating Allah and His Messenger with their argument.

As for what this day is, alluded to by His saying: "The day they see the angels," the commentators (المفسرون) have mentioned that it is the Day of Resurrection. However, what the context provides, along with the verses describing the day of death and what follows it, such as His saying: وَلَوْ تَرَى إِذِ الظَّالِمُونَ فِي غَمَرَاتِ الْمَوْتِ وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ بَاسِطُو أَيْدِيهِمْ أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ الْهُونِ — "...Were you to see when the wrongdoers are in the throes of death, and the angels stretch out their hands [saying], ‘Surrender your souls! Today you shall be requited with a humiliating punishment...’" (Al-An'am: 93), and His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ ظَالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ قَالُوا فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ قَالُوا كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ قَالُوا أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ اللَّهِ وَاسِعَةً فَتُهَاجِرُوا فِيهَا — "Indeed, those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves, they [the angels] say, ‘What state were you in?’ They say, ‘We were oppressed in the land.’ They say, ‘Was not Allah’s earth vast so that you might have migrated in it?’" (An-Nisa: 97), among other verses—

—is that what is meant by it is death, which is termed in the parlance of the Quran as the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection. For in the verses, there is definitive proof that they see the angels and speak with them after death before the Day of Resurrection. What is required—according to the nature of disputation—in answering one who denies the vision of angels is to mention to him the first day he will see them in a way that grieves him, which is the day of death, rather than disputing by mentioning their vision on the Day of Resurrection and his saying to them: "Keep off!" when he had already seen them before that and been punished at their hands for a long duration; this is evident.

Thus, it appears that this verse and the two following verses look toward their state in the intermediate realm (البرزخ), describing their vision of the angels therein, the nullification of their deeds therein, and the state of the people of Paradise therein.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَدِمْنَا إِلَى مَا عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ هَبَاءً مَنْثُورًا — "Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then render them as scattered dust" (Al-Furqan: 23). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Work (العمل) is every act (فعل) that proceeds from an animal with intention; thus, it is more specific than 'act' (الفعل), because 'act' may be attributed to animals from whom an act occurs without intention, and it may be attributed to inanimate objects, whereas 'work' is rarely attributed to such. 'Work' was not used for animals except in their saying: 'working oxen' (البقر العوامل)." End quote.

He also said: "Dust (الهباء) is the fine particles of soil and what is scattered in the air, which does not appear except within the sunlight in a window-gap." End quote. And scattering (النثر) is dispersal.

The meaning is: We turned toward every work they performed—and work is that by which a human lives after death—and We dispersed it in a dispersal such that they cannot benefit from it, like scattered dust. The speech is based on an allegory (التمثيل), likening the dominance of Divine Subjugation over all their works—which they performed for the happiness of life—and their nullification such that they do not affect the happiness of their eternal life in any way, to a sovereign who overcomes his enemy, enters his house after prevailing over him, destroys the house, demolishes the landmarks, and burns the goods and furniture, thus annihilating every trace and essence of it.

There is no contradiction between what the verse indicates regarding the nullification (حبط) of deeds on that day and what other verses indicate—that their deeds were nullified when they performed them in the world due to their disbelief and guilt. For the meaning of nullification after death is the manifestation of the nullification to them after it had been hidden from them in the world. An extensive discussion on the meaning of nullification (الحبط) has already been provided in the second volume of this book; so refer to it.

His saying, the Exalted: أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا — "On that day the inhabitants of paradise will be in a better abode and a better resting place" (Al-Furqan: 24). What is meant by "the inhabitants of paradise" are the God-conscious (المتقون), for His saying preceded a few verses ago: قُلْ أَذَلِكَ خَيْرٌ أَمْ جَنَّةُ الْخُلْدِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ — "Say, ‘Is that better, or the everlasting paradise promised to the God-conscious?’" (Al-Furqan: 15). The "abode" (المستقر) and "resting place" (المقيل) are nouns of place derived from "stability" (الاستقرار), whose meaning is clear, and from "midday rest" (القيلولة), which is relaxation in the middle of the day, whether accompanied by sleep or not—according to what has been said—and there is no sleep in Paradise.

The words "better" (خير) and "more excellent" (أحسن) are stripped of the sense of comparison (التفضيل), as in His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ — "...and that is easier for Him" (Ar-Rum: 27), and His saying: مَا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللَّهْوِ — "What is with Allah is better than diversion..." (Al-Jumu'ah: 11); so it has been said. It is not far-fetched to say that the elative form (af‘al) or what is in its meaning like khayr—based on what we preferred, that it is an intensive adjective (صفة مشبهة) indicating preference by its root matter rather than its morphological form—is not stripped of the sense of comparison in such instances. The point of interest in that is that since they chose polytheism and guilt and deemed that good, and its necessary consequence is the Fire in the Hereafter, they have affirmed for it a "goodness" and "excellence." Thus, they were countered with the fact that Paradise and what is in it are better and more excellent even according to the necessary implication of their own words; so they ought to choose it over the Fire and choose faith over disbelief in any case. It is also said that the comparison is based on sarcasm.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّمَاءُ بِالْغَمَامِ وَنُزِّلَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ تَنْزِيلًا — "The day when the sky is rent asunder with the clouds and the angels are sent down in a [grand] descent" (Al-Furqan: 25). It appears that the adverbial qualifier is governed by an omitted verb, and the meaning is: "And remember the day such-and-such," for they shall see the angels therein as well. This day is the Day of Resurrection, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمَنِ — "On that day the true sovereignty will belong to the All-beneficent..." (Al-Furqan: 26). Other views regarding the governor of the adverbial qualifier have been mentioned, but there is no benefit in recounting them.

"Is rent asunder" (تَشَقَّقُ) is originally tatashaqqaqu from the paradigm of tafa‘‘ul, from shaqq meaning to breach or tear, and tashaqquq is opening up. "The clouds" (الغمام) are named so because they veil the light of the sun, derived from ghamm meaning to cover or veil.

The preposition bi (ب) in His saying: "the sky is rent asunder with the clouds" (تَشَقَّقُ السَّمَاءُ بِالْغَمَامِ) is either for accompaniment (الملابسة), meaning the sky opens up while being accompanied by clouds, i.e., becoming cloudy; or it is in the sense of "from" (عن), meaning it opens up from the clouds, i.e., from the direction of the clouds or their rending.

In any case, the apparent meaning of the verse is that the sky rends asunder on the Day of Resurrection along with the clouds that veil it, and the angels who are its inhabitants descend from it, so they witness them. Thus, the verse is close in meaning to His saying elsewhere: وَانْشَقَّتِ السَّمَاءُ فَهِيَ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاهِيَةٌ وَالْمَلَكُ عَلَى أَرْجَائِهَا — "The sky will be rent asunder, for on that day it will be frail, and the angels will be all over it" (Al-Haqqah: 16-17).

It is not far-fetched that the speech is a metaphor (كناية) for the lifting of the cloud of ignorance and the emergence of the celestial world—which is from the Unseen—and the emergence of its inhabitants, who are the angels, and their descent to the earthly world, the dwelling place of man.

It is also said: the meaning is that the clouds rend the sky, which is what He mentions in His saying: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ فِي ظُلَلٍ مِنَ الْغَمَامِ وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ وَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ وَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ — "Do they await anything but that Allah should come to them in the shadows of the clouds, with the angels, and the matter be decided? And to Allah all matters are returned" (Al-Baqarah: 210). Discussion on the interpretation of this verse has already passed.

The expression of the event with "rending asunder" (التشقق) rather than "opening" or similar terms is for intimidation (التهويل), as is the nunation (التنوين) in His saying: "in a [grand] descent" (تَنْزِيلًا) to indicate magnification (التفخيم).

His saying, the Exalted: الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمَنِ وَكَانَ يَوْمًا عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ عَسِيرًا — "On that day the true sovereignty will belong to the All-beneficent, and it will be a day of hardship for the faithless" (Al-Furqan: 26). That is, absolute sovereignty on that day is a fixed truth for the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), due to the nullification of causes (الأسباب) and the cessation of the various links between them and their effects. It has been mentioned more than once that what is meant by this on the Day of Resurrection is the manifestation that sovereignty and judgment belong to Allah and the command is His alone, and that there is no independence in any of the causes, contrary to what appeared from the outward state of affairs in the worldly realm before the rising of the Hour and the return of everything to Him, the Exalted.

His saying: وَكَانَ يَوْمًا عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ عَسِيرًا — "and it will be a day of hardship for the faithless." The reason for this is their reliance on the outward appearances of causes, their clinging to the vanishing and perishing earthly life, and their disconnection from the True Cause who is the Master of Sovereignty in reality, and from their eternal, everlasting life. Thus, they reach that day with no shelter and no refuge.

Accordingly, "sovereignty" (الملك) is the subject (مبتدأ) and "the true" (الحق) is its predicate (خبر), made definite to signify restriction (الحصر). "On that day" (يومئذ) is an adverbial qualifier for the establishment of the predicate for the subject. The benefit of this restriction is to indicate the manifestation of the reality of the matter on that day, for the reality of sovereignty belongs to Allah, the Glorified, always; it only differs on the Day of Resurrection from other times by the cessation of formal sovereignty from things therein and its establishment for them in other times.

Some have said: "sovereignty" (الملك) is in the sense of ownership (المالكية) and "on that day" is related to it, while "the true" is the predicate of "sovereignty." It is also said: "on that day" is related to an omitted word which is an attribute for "the true." It is also said: what is meant by "on that day" is the Day of Allah. It is also said: "on that day" is the predicate for "sovereignty" and "the true" is an attribute for the subject. These are weak opinions of no use.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ يَعَضُّ الظَّالِمُ عَلَى يَدَيْهِ يَقُولُ يَا لَيْتَنِي اتَّخَذْتُ مَعَ الرَّسُولِ سَبِيلًا — "A day when the wrongdoer will bite his hands, saying, ‘I wish I had followed the Prophet’s way!’" (Al-Furqan: 27). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Biting (العض) is gripping with the teeth. He, the Exalted, said: عَضُّوا عَلَيْكُمُ الْأَنَامِلَ — '...they bite their fingertips at you...' (Aal-Imran: 119) and: وَيَوْمَ يَعَضُّ الظَّالِمُ — 'A day when the wrongdoer will bite...' This is an expression of regret (الندم), as it is what people customarily do at such a time." End quote. Therefore, he wishes at that moment for the mandatory work that he missed, as Allah, the Exalted, recounted their saying: يَا وَيْلَتَى لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أَتَّخِذْ فُلَانًا خَلِيلًا — "Woe to me! I wish I had not taken so-and-so for a friend!" (Al-Furqan: 28).

It appears that what is meant by "the wrongdoer" (الظالم) is the genus, which is everyone who was not guided by the guidance of the Messenger. Likewise, what is meant by "the Messenger" (الرسول) is the genus, even if "the wrongdoer" applies according to the specific context to the wrongdoers of this nation and "the Messenger" to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The meaning is: And remember the day the wrongdoer regrets with a severe regret, saying out of the intensity of his regret: "I wish I had taken with the Messenger a path," any path toward guidance it might have been.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا وَيْلَتَى لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أَتَّخِذْ فُلَانًا خَلِيلًا — "Woe to me! I wish I had not taken so-and-so for a friend!" (Al-Furqan: 28). This is the completion of the wish of the wrongdoer who regrets his wrongdoing. So-and-so (فلان) is a metonym (كناية) for a masculine proper noun, and so-and-so (فلانة) for a feminine proper noun. Al-Raghib said: "Fulan and fulanah are metonyms for a human being, while al-fulan and al-fulanah—with the definite article—are metonyms for animals." End quote.

The meaning is: O my woe—O my destruction—I wish I had not taken so-and-so—referring to whoever he took as a friend whom he consulted, listened to, and imitated—as an intimate friend (خليلاً).

Some have mentioned that "so-and-so" in the verse is a metonym for Satan (الشيطان), perhaps in view of what is in the following verse regarding Satan's desertion of man; however, the context does not support this.

Among the subtle expressions is His saying in the previous verse: "I wish I had taken..." etc., and in this verse: "Woe to me! I wish I had not taken..." etc. For in this, there is a subtle progression in the call and the plea for help. The omission of the vocative (المنادي) in the previous verse suggests that he seeks any savior to save him from the wretchedness he is in. The mention of "woe" (الويل) after that—in this verse—indicates that it has become clear to him that nothing at all will save him from the punishment except destruction and annihilation; therefore, he called upon "woe."

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ أَضَلَّني عَنِ الذِّكْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ جَاءَنِي وَكَانَ الشَّيْطَانُ لِلْإِنْسَانِ خَذُولًا — "He certainly led me astray from the Reminder after it had come to me, and Satan is a deserter of man" (Al-Furqan: 29). This is the justification for the preceding wish. What is meant by "the Reminder" (الذكر) is either everything the messengers brought in general, or specifically the heavenly books, and it applies according to the context to the Quran.

His saying: وَكَانَ الشَّيْطَانُ لِلْإِنْسَانِ خَذُولًا — "and Satan is a deserter of man" is from the speech of Him, the Exalted. It is also possible that it is a completion of the wrongdoer's speech, mentioned by him in sorrow and grief.

Desertion (الخذلان) is the abandonment of one who is expected to help. His desertion is that he promises man that he will help him against every disliked thing if he clings to causes and forgets his Lord. Then, when the causes are severed by the manifestation of Divine Subjugation—partially on the day of death and totally on the Day of Resurrection—he deserts him and surrenders him to wretchedness. He, the Exalted, said: كَمَثَلِ الشَّيْطَانِ إِذْ قَالَ لِلْإِنْسَانِ اكْفُرْ فَلَمَّا كَفَرَ قَالَ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِنْكَ — "Like Satan when he said to man, ‘Disbelieve.’ Then when he disbelieved, he said, ‘I am indeed quit of you...’" (Al-Hashr: 16). And He said regarding what He recounts from Satan on the Day of Resurrection: مَا أَنَا بِمُصْرِخِكُمْ وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُصْرِخِيَّ إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ بِمَا أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِنْ قَبْلُ — "...I cannot help you, nor can you help me. Indeed I disavow your having taken me as a partner before..." (Ibrahim: 22).

In these three verses, there is an intimation, indeed a proof, that the primary cause for the straying of the people of misguidance is the guardianship (ولاية) of the people of whims and the allies of Satan; observation supports this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَ الرَّسُولُ يَا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هَذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُورًا — "And the Apostle says, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people consigned this Quran to oblivion’" (Al-Furqan: 30). What is meant by "the Apostle" is Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by the context of the mention of the Quran. He was referred to as "the Apostle" to affirm his messengership and to spite those who impugned his mission and his Book. Hajr (الهجر) is abandonment.

The apparent context is that His saying: "And the Apostle says..." etc., is coordinated (معطوف) with "the wrongdoer will bite." The saying is among what the Apostle says on the Day of Resurrection to his Lord by way of lament and complaint. Accordingly, the expression in the past tense is due to the certainty of its occurrence. What is meant by "the people" (القوم) is the Arabs in general, or rather the nation in general, considering its disbelievers and sinners.

As for it being an inception (استئناف) or coordinated with His saying: "Those who do not expect to encounter Us say," and that what occurred between them is a parenthetical statement (اعتراض), this is far from the context. According to that view, the word "said" (قال) would be in its literal past sense, and what is meant by "the people" would be those who impugned his mission and slandered his Book.

Similar in weakness is the view of some that mahjur (المهجور) is from hajr in the sense of "delirium" (الهذيان); this is obvious.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذَلِكَ جَعَلْنَا لِكُلِّ نَبِيٍّ عَدُوًّا مِنَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ وَكَفَى بِرَبِّكَ هَادِيًا وَنَصِيرًا — "That is how We have appointed for every prophet an enemy from among the guilty, and your Lord suffices as a guide and helper" (Al-Furqan: 31). That is: just as We made these guilty ones an enemy to you, so too We made for every prophet an enemy from among them. That is, this is from Our established sunnah (سنتنا) regarding the prophets and their nations; so let not what you encounter of their enmity grieve you, nor let it be hard upon you. In this, there is consolation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The meaning of "appointing the enemy from the guilty" is that Allah requited them for their sins by sealing their hearts; thus, they opposed the truth and hated the one calling to it, who is the Prophet. So their enmity has an attribution to Him, the Exalted, by way of requital.

His saying: وَكَفَى بِرَبِّكَ هَادِيًا وَنَصِيرًا — "and your Lord suffices as a guide and helper" means—according to what the context provides—let not the matter of their opposition and enmity terrify you, and do not fear them regarding the guidance of the people and the penetration of your religion among them. For your Lord is sufficient for you; He suffices as a Guide who guides whoever among the people deserves guidance and is prepared for it, even if these people disbelieve and are defiant. The guidance of the people is not contingent upon their guidance. And He suffices as a Helper who helps you and helps your religion with which He sent you, even if these people abandon it and do not help you or your religion. The sentence is intended to demonstrate independence from them.

Thus, it has become clear that the beginning of the verse is intended to console the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and its end is to show independence from the guilty among his people. In His saying: "and your Lord suffices," where the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية) is used, suffixed to the second-person pronoun, rather than saying "and Allah suffices," there is a support for him.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from the book al-Jannah wa al-Nar, with its chain of transmission from Jabir ibn Yazid al-Ju‘fi from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): In a tradition in which he mentions the seizing of the soul of a disbeliever, he said: فإذا بلغت الحلقوم ضربت الملائكة وجهه ودبره وقيل : «أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ الْهُونِ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللهِ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ ـ وَكُنْتُمْ عَنْ آيَاتِهِ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ» وذلك قوله : «يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلائِكَةَ لا بُشْرى يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ ـ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْراً مَحْجُوراً» فيقولون حراما عليكم الجنة محرما. — "When it reaches the throat, the angels strike his face and his back, and it is said: 'Surrender your souls! Today you shall be requited with a humiliating punishment for what you used to say about Allah untruly, and because you used to be disdainful of His signs.' And that is His saying: 'The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day, and they will say, "Keep off!"' So they [the angels] say: 'Forbidden to you is Paradise, prohibited.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) that he said: الهباء ريح الغبار يسطع ثم يذهب فلا يبقى منه شيء ـ فجعل الله أعمالهم كذلك. — "The haba’ (dust) is the wind-blown dust that rises and then vanishes so that nothing remains of it—so Allah made their deeds like that."

In the same source, Samawayh narrated in his Fawa’id from Salim, the client of Abu Hudhayfah, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: ليجاء يوم القيامة بقوم معهم حسنات مثال جبال تهامة ـ حتى إذا جيء بهم جعل الله تعالى أعمالهم هباء ثم قذفهم في النار. — "On the Day of Resurrection, a people will be brought having righteous deeds (حسنات) like the mountains of Tihamah—until when they are brought, Allah, the Exalted, will render their deeds as dust and then cast them into the Fire." Salim said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah, describe these people to us." He said: كانوا يصلون ويصومون ويأخذون سنة من الليل ـ ولكن كانوا إذا عرض عليهم شيء من الحرام وثبوا عليه ـ فأدحض الله تعالى أعمالهم. — "They used to pray and fast and take a portion of the night [for worship]—but whenever something of the forbidden (الحرام) was presented to them, they would pounce upon it—so Allah, the Exalted, nullified their deeds."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Sulayman ibn Khalid, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَقَدِمْنَا إِلَى مَا عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ هَبَاءً مَنْثُورًا — "Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then render them as scattered dust." He said: أما والله لقد كانت أعمالهم ـ أشد بياضا من القباطي ـ ولكن كانوا إذا عرض لهم حرام لم يدعوه. — "By Allah, their deeds were whiter than fine Egyptian linen—but whenever a forbidden thing was presented to them, they would not leave it."

I say: This meaning is narrated in it and in other sources from him and from his father (peace be upon them) through more than one path.

In al-Kafi as well, with its chain of transmission from Abd al-A‘la, and by another chain from Suwayd ibn Ghaflah, who said: Amir al-Mu’minin (peace be upon him) said: In a tradition regarding the placing of a believer in his grave: ثم يفسحان يعني الملكين في قبره مد بصره ـ ثم يفتحان له بابا إلى الجنة ويقولان له : نم قرير العين نوم الشاب الناعم فإن الله يقول : «أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلاً» — "Then they—meaning the two angels—widen his grave as far as his eye can see—then they open for him a door to Paradise and say to him: 'Sleep with a delighted eye, the sleep of a pampered youth, for Allah says: "On that day the inhabitants of paradise will be in a better abode and a better resting place."'"

I say: The narration—as you see—makes the verse one of the verses of the intermediate realm (البرزخ), and alludes by the saying: "and it is said to him: 'Sleep...'" etc., to the point of the expression in the verse with "resting place" (المقيل); so let this be noted.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu‘aym narrated by way of al-Kalbi from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas, who said: Uqbah ibn Abi Mu‘ayt would not return from a journey except that he would prepare food and invite all the people of Mecca to it. He used to frequently sit with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and was pleased with his speech—but wretchedness overcame him.

One day he returned from a journey and prepared food—then he invited the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) to his food. He said: ما أنا بالذي آكل من طعامك ـ حتى تشهد أن لا إله إلا الله وأني رسول الله — "I am not one to eat from your food until you testify that there is no god but Allah and that I am the Messenger of Allah." He said: "Eat, O son of my brother." He said: "I am not one to do so until you say it." So he testified to that and he [the Prophet] ate from his food.

News of this reached Ubayy ibn Khalaf, so he came to him and said: "Have you turned apostate, O Uqbah?"—and he was his intimate friend—he said: "No, by Allah, I have not turned apostate, but a man entered upon me—and he refused to eat from my food unless I testified for him—so I was ashamed that he should leave my house before eating—so I testified for him and he ate." He said: "I am not one to be pleased with you until you go to him and spit in his face." So Uqbah did that. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to him: لا ألقاك خارجا من مكة ـ إلا علوت رأسك بالسيف — "I shall not meet you outside of Mecca except that I shall strike your head with the sword." Uqbah was captured on the day of Badr and was executed—and none of the prisoners were killed on that day except him.

I say: It has been mentioned in more than one narration regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَقُولُ يَا لَيْتَنِي اتَّخَذْتُ مَعَ الرَّسُولِ سَبِيلًا — "saying, ‘I wish I had followed the Prophet’s way!’" that "the way" (السبيل) is Ali (peace be upon him). This is from the inner depth (بطن) of the Quran or of the nature of application (الجري), and is not part of the formal interpretation (التفسير) at all.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ جُمْلَةً وَٰحِدَةً ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِۦ فُؤَادَكَ ۖ وَرَتَّلْنَـٰهُ تَرْتِيلًا (٣٢)
The faithless say, ‘Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?’ So it was, that We may strengthen your heart with it, and We have recited it [to you] in a measured tone. (32)
وَلَا يَأْتُونَكَ بِمَثَلٍ إِلَّا جِئْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَحْسَنَ تَفْسِيرًا (٣٣)
They do not bring you any representation but that We bring you the truth [in reply to them] and the best exposition. (33)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُحْشَرُونَ عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا (٣٤)
Those who will be mustered [fallen] on their faces toward hell, they are the worse situated and further astray from the [right] way. (34)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَجَعَلْنَا مَعَهُۥٓ أَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ وَزِيرًا (٣٥)
Certainly We gave Moses the Book and We made Aaron, his brother, accompany him as a minister. (35)
فَقُلْنَا ٱذْهَبَآ إِلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَدَمَّرْنَـٰهُمْ تَدْمِيرًا (٣٦)
Then We said, ‘Let the two of you go to the people who have denied Our signs.’ Then We destroyed them utterly. (36)
وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ لَّمَّا كَذَّبُوا۟ ٱلرُّسُلَ أَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ لِلنَّاسِ ءَايَةً ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (٣٧)
And Noah’s people, We drowned them when they impugned the apostles, and We made them a sign for mankind, and We have prepared for the wrongdoers a painful punishment. (37)
وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَا۟ وَأَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلرَّسِّ وَقُرُونًۢا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ كَثِيرًا (٣٨)
And ‘Ad and Thamud, and the people of Rass, and many generations between them. (38)
وَكُلًّا ضَرَبْنَا لَهُ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ ۖ وَكُلًّا تَبَّرْنَا تَتْبِيرًا (٣٩)
For each of them We drew examples, and each We destroyed utterly. (39)
وَلَقَدْ أَتَوْا۟ عَلَى ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِىٓ أُمْطِرَتْ مَطَرَ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۚ أَفَلَمْ يَكُونُوا۟ يَرَوْنَهَا ۚ بَلْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَرْجُونَ نُشُورًا (٤٠)
Certainly they must have passed the town on which an evil shower was rained. Have they not seen it? Rather, they did not expect resurrection [to happen]. (40)

(Commentary)

This section relates another objection from the criticisms they leveled against the Quran—namely, that it was not revealed all at once—and the response to it.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً» — And the faithless say, ‘Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?’ (25:32). Those intended by this are the polytheists of the Arabs who rejected the call of the Quran, as seen in their previous defamation recounted in His saying: «وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ هَذا إِلَّا إِفْكٌ افْتَراهُ» — The faithless say, ‘This is nothing but a lie that he has fabricated...’ (25:4).

Regarding His saying: «لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً» — ‘Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?’, it has been mentioned previously that the terms "sending down" (الإنزال) and "gradual revelation" (التنزيل) differ in that the former implies a single instance while the latter implies gradualness. However, some have noted that the term "gradual revelation" (التنزيل) in this verse is stripped of the meaning of gradualness because it would lead to a contradiction; for if gradualness were intended, the meaning would be: "Why was the Quran not divided into parts all at once?" and division contradicts being a single whole. Rather, the meaning is: "Why was the Quran not sent down upon him in one instance, undivided, just as the Torah, the Gospel, and the Psalms were sent down?"

However, it should be known that the descent of the Torah, for example—as is apparent from what is understood from the Quran—was in a single instance in a written book on tablets. The Quran, on the other hand, was sent down to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله through reception (التلقي) from Allah by the mediation of the Trustworthy Spirit (الروح الأمين), just as a listener receives speech from a speaker. While the instantaneous granting and receiving of a written book necessitates the simultaneity of its beginning and its end, the process of recitation and hearing does not negate gradualness between its parts and portions. Indeed, it is necessary for the reciter to deliver it and the listener to receive it, taking it from its beginning to its end, bit by bit.

These people were only proposing the revelation of the Quran all at once based on what they were witnessing or hearing regarding the manner of the revelation of the inspiration (الوحي) to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, which was the reception of the verses with their specific wordings from the tongue of the Angel of Revelation. Their proposal was that since the Angel of Revelation recites it to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله chapter after chapter and verse after verse, and he receives it as such, let him recite all of it at one time and let the Prophet receive it at one time, even if the recitation and reception were to last for a period of time. This meaning is more consistent with the term "gradual revelation" (التنزيل) which denotes a process.

As for the claim that their intention in proposing its revelation "all at once" was for it to descend as a written book in a single instance—as the Torah, Gospel, and Psalms were revealed according to what was known to them—there is no indication of this in the speech reported from them. Furthermore, they were not believers in these heavenly books such that they would accept their instantaneous revelation as a premise.

In any case, their saying: «لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً» — ‘Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?’ is an objection to the Quran regarding the mode of its revelation. They intended by this that it is not a heavenly book revealed from Allah, the Glorified. For if it were a heavenly book containing a divine religion (دين سماوي) that Allah desires from the people, and He had sent a messenger to convey it to them, then the religion contained therein would be a complete religion with known parts, established principles and branches, and gathered obligations and traditions; and the book containing it would have its parts organized and composed one upon another.

But it is not so; rather, it consists of scattered sayings that he brings forth during different events and disparate incidents. Perhaps an event occurs, and at that time he brings forth some speech related to it, calling its arranged sentences "divine verses," attributing them to Allah and claiming they are a Quran revealed to him from Allah, the Glorified. This is nothing but his own labor from time to time when events occur, whereby he invents a saying and fabricates it against Allah; he is nothing but a Sabian man who has strayed from the path. This is the formulation of their objection based on what is gathered from the objection and the response.

His saying, the Exalted: «كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ وَرَتَّلْناهُ تَرْتِيلاً وَلا يَأْتُونَكَ بِمَثَلٍ إِلَّا جِئْناكَ بِالْحَقِّ وَأَحْسَنَ تَفْسِيراً» — So it is, that We may strengthen your heart therewith, and We have recited it in a measured tone. They do not bring you any similitude but that We bring you the truth and a better explanation. (25:32-33). Firmness (الثبات) is the opposite of vanishing. "To make firm" (الإثبات) and "to strengthen/stabilize" (التثبيت) carry the same meaning, the difference between them being instantaneousness versus gradualness. The "heart" (الفؤاد) refers to the "intellect/heart" (القلب), and what is intended by it—as mentioned more than once—is the perceiving faculty of the human being, which is his very self (نفسه). "Measured recitation" (الترتيل)—as they have said—is "deliberate delivery" (الترسيل) and bringing one thing after another. "Explanation" (التفسير)—as al-Raghib said—is the exaggeration in manifesting a conceptual meaning, just as "uncovering" (الفسر) is the manifestation of a conceptual meaning.

The apparent context suggests that His saying: «كَذلِكَ» — "So it is" is connected to an omitted verb for which His saying: «لِنُثَبِّتَ» — "that We may strengthen" provides the reason, and to which His saying: «وَرَتَّلْناهُ» — "and We have recited it" is coordinated. The estimation is: "We revealed it—meaning the Quran—in this manner—meaning in scattered installments and not all at once—so that We may strengthen your heart therewith." The opinion of some that «كَذلِكَ» is part of the speech of the faithless is very weak.

Thus, His saying: «كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ» — "So it is, that We may strengthen your heart therewith" is a complete explanation for the reason the Quran was revealed in scattered installments. The explanation for this is that teaching a science—especially one linked to action—by the teacher delivering its issues one after another to the student until its chapters and sections are complete, only results in a certain acquisition of the forms of those issues in the student's mind, being stored in a way that he may refer to them when the need arises. However, their stabilization in the soul (النفس) such that the soul grows upon them and their desired effects are produced, requires the presence of actual need, the supervision of the action, and the arrival of its time.

There is a difference between a teaching physician, for example, delivering a medical issue to a student of medicine merely as a lecture, and delivering it to him in the presence of a patient afflicted with the disease being discussed while he treats him, thereby matching what he says with what he does.

From here it becomes clear that delivering any scientific insight at the time of pressing need and the arrival of the time for action to the one intended for education and training is more firmly established in the soul, more impactful on the heart, more stable, and more perfectly rooted in the mind. This is especially true for the gnoses (المعارف) to which the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides, for the primordial nature is only prepared for acceptance and ready for submission when it senses the need.

Furthermore, the gnoses contained in the Islamic call articulated by the Quran are only practical laws and rulings, and individual and social regulations that bring felicity to human life, built upon virtuous ethics linked to the universal divine gnoses (المعارف الكلية الإلهية) which, through analysis, lead back to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. Just as monotheism, through synthesis, leads to them, and then to ethics and practical rulings.

Therefore, the best teaching and most perfect training is that these high gnoses be delivered gradually, distributed over the occurring events that involve various types of needs, explaining the true belief, virtuous character, and legislated practical ruling associated with them, along with what pertains to them of causes for reflection and admonition from the stories of the past and the end of the profligate, and the defiance of the tyrannical and the arrogant.

This is the way of the Quranic explanations deposited in its revealed verses, as He, the Exalted, said: «وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً» — ‘We have sent the Quran in installments so that you may recite it to the people at intervals, and We have sent it down in discrete revelations.’ (17:106). This is what is intended by His saying, the Exalted: «كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ» — "So it is, that We may strengthen your heart therewith", and Allah knows best.

Yes, one point remains: that the separation of the parts of instruction and delivering them to the student with delay and deliberation might spoil the purpose of teaching because the effect of the previous part is cut off before the subsequent part joins it, and the ambition and resolve to master the subjects might fail. For in the connection of the parts of a single science to one another, there is a support for the mind and a preparation for the understanding toward deep comprehension (التفقه) and mastery that cannot be achieved without it.

Allah, the Exalted, answered this by His saying: «وَرَتَّلْناهُ تَرْتِيلاً» — "and We have recited it in a measured tone." Its meaning, according to what the context provides, is that these instructions, despite their revelation in scattered installments, We have followed one with another and revealed one after another such that the links are not nullified and the effects of the parts are not severed. Thus, the purpose of teaching is not spoiled; rather, they are chapters and verses revealed one after another, ordered and measured (مرتلة).

Moreover, there is another matter: the Quran is a book of explanation and argumentation (احتجاج). It argues against the ally and the opponent regarding what is problematic for them or what they raise as problems against the truth and reality through doubt and objection. It explains to them what has become confused for them regarding the gnoses and wisdoms found in previous nations and religions, and how their scholars interpreted them by distorting the words from their proper places. This becomes apparent by comparing what the idolaters believed regarding Allah, the angels, the jinn, and the holy men, and what occurred in the Two Testaments (the Bible) of the stories of the prophets and the gnoses they spread regarding the Origin and the Return (المبدإ والمعاد), to what the Quran explains in that regard.

This type of argumentation and explanation cannot fulfill its right except through gradual revelation according to the doubts that appeared from them and the questions that were directed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله gradually, and the allurements they brought against the believers or their people bit by bit and time after time.

To this, His saying, the Exalted, refers: «وَلا يَأْتُونَكَ بِمَثَلٍ إِلَّا جِئْناكَ بِالْحَقِّ وَأَحْسَنَ تَفْسِيراً» — "They do not bring you any similitude but that We bring you the truth and a better explanation." The similitude (المثل) is the description—meaning, they do not bring you a description regarding you or others by which they deviate from the truth or whose explanation they worsen, except that We bring you that which is the truth regarding it or what is the best of ways in its explanation. For what they bring is either pure falsehood, which the truth repels, or a truth distorted from its place, which the better explanation restores to its proper level and rectifies.

It has become clear from what preceded that His saying: «كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ» — "So it is, that We may strengthen your heart therewith" until His saying: «وَأَحْسَنَ تَفْسِيراً» — "and a better explanation" is a response to their saying: «لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً» — ‘Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?’ from two aspects:

First: An explanation of the reason pertaining to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, which is the strengthening of his heart through gradual revelation.

Second: An explanation of the reason pertaining to the people, which is the clarification of the truth in the false similitudes and descriptions they bring against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the explanation with the best of ways regarding the truth they bring that has been changed from its face and distorted from its place.

Attached to this response is His saying immediately following: «الَّذِينَ يُحْشَرُونَ عَلى وُجُوهِهِمْ إِلى جَهَنَّمَ أُوْلئِكَ شَرٌّ مَكاناً وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلاً» — "Those who will be mustered on their faces toward hell—they are in a worse plight and further astray from the way." (25:34). It is like a completion of the response, as will be explained.

It has also become clear that the three verses are all driven toward a single purpose, which is the response to the defamation they brought against the Quran. However, the commentators have separated the contents of the three verses, making His saying: «كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ» the response to their saying: «لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً», and His saying: «وَرَتَّلْناهُ تَرْتِيلاً» a report about its deliberate delivery in revelation or in recitation to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله without connection to what preceded it.

They made His saying: «وَلا يَأْتُونَكَ بِمَثَلٍ» etc., like an explanation for His saying: «كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ» and a clarification of the manner of strengthening his heart صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Some made it specifically regarding the similitude they struck for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and that Allah explained the truth in it and brought the best explanation; and other things were said. They made His saying: «الَّذِينَ يُحْشَرُونَ» (the verse) entirely foreign to the purpose of the two preceding verses.

Reflection on what we have presented in directing the content of the first two verses and what will come regarding the meaning of the third verse clarifies the invalidity of all that, and shows that the three verses together possess a single purpose, which is the response to the criticism they raised against the Quran regarding its gradual revelation.

They also mentioned that the response to their defamation and proposal with His saying: «كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ» is a response by mentioning some of the benefits of the divided revelation, and that there are other benefits besides what Allah, the Exalted, mentioned. They have cited other benefits which they added to what occurred in the verse:

Among them: That the heavenly books prior to the Quran were only revealed all at once because they were revealed to prophets who could read and write, so they were revealed to them as a single written whole. The Quran, however, was revealed to an unlettered prophet (نبي أمي) who neither writes nor reads, and therefore it was revealed in parts.

Among them: That the previous books did not have their miraculous nature (إعجاز) as the witness of their authenticity and the proof of their being from Allah, the Exalted. As for the Quran, the evidence of its authenticity and the sign of its being from Allah, the Exalted, is its miraculous composition (نظمه المعجز) which remains throughout the ages, realized in every one of its parts, even in the measure of the shortest chapter, according to the challenge (التحدي) issued by it. There is no doubt that the pivot of the miracle is the correspondence to what the circumstances necessitate, and from the necessity of the renewal of circumstances comes the renewal of what corresponds to them.

Among them: That in the Quran there is the abrogating (الناسخ) — the verse that replaces a previous ruling — and the abrogated (المنسوخ) — the verse whose ruling has been replaced — and it is not possible to combine them due to the opposition and contradiction. In it is that which is an answer to questions they asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and in it is that which is a denunciation of some of what occurred, and in it is a narration of some of what happened, and in it is that which contains news of what would come in the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, such as the news of the conquest of Mecca and the entry into the Sacred Mosque, and the news of the victory of the Romans over the Persians, among other benefits. Thus, wisdom necessitated its revelation in parts.

These are weak points that do not necessitate the impossibility of revelation all at once:

As for the first point: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله being unlettered, neither reading nor writing, does not prevent revelation all at once, for there were those with him who would write and memorize it. Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, promised him that He would protect him from forgetting and preserve the Reminder (الذكر) revealed to him, as He said: «سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى» — "We shall make you recite, and you will not forget" (87:6), and He said: «إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ» — "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it." (15:9), and He said: «إِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِيزٌ لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ» — "Indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it" (41:41-42). His power, the Exalted, to preserve His book is the same whether it is revealed in one instance or gradually.

As for the second point: Just as divided speech is accompanied by circumstances that necessitate certain things in its composition—which if the speech contains them, it is eloquent, and otherwise not—likewise, speech as a whole, even if it is a book, is accompanied in its chapters and parts by circumstances that have requirements; if it corresponds to them, it is eloquent, and otherwise not. Thus, eloquence is not dependent on anything other than the book revealed in one instance and the speech gathered as a single whole.

As for the third point: Abrogation (النسخ) is not a nullification of the previous ruling, but rather an explanation of the end of its term. Thus, it is possible to combine the two rulings—the abrogated and the abrogating—by indicating that the first ruling is limited and temporary if the interest necessitates that.

It is also possible to provide the explanation of the issues they will ask about beforehand so that they do not need to ask; and if they were to ask about any of them, they would be referred to the previous explanation. Likewise, it is possible to provide the mention of what is a denunciation of what was, or a narration of what happened, or news of some of the unseen matters; none of that is impossible to provide beforehand, as is apparent.

Moreover, the division of revelation for some of these wisdoms and interests is part of the "strengthening of the heart," so these mentioned points are not independent reasons on their own.

The truth is that the explanation occurring in the verse is a complete and comprehensive explanation with which there is no need for any of these points at all.

His saying, the Exalted: «الَّذِينَ يُحْشَرُونَ عَلى وُجُوهِهِمْ إِلى جَهَنَّمَ أُوْلئِكَ شَرٌّ مَكاناً وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلاً» — "Those who will be mustered on their faces toward hell—they are in a worse plight and further astray from the way." (25:34). The connection of the verse to what precedes it, given its context, indicates that these critics of the Quran concluded from their criticism something inappropriate for the status of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, so they mentioned him describing him with an evil position and straying from the path. Allah, the Exalted, did not mention it within what He recounted of their speech regarding the Quran to protect the status of prophethood from being mentioned with evil; rather, He pointed to that in what He provided in this verse of the rebuttal against them by way of metonymy (التكنية).

Thus, His saying: «الَّذِينَ يُحْشَرُونَ عَلى وُجُوهِهِمْ إِلى جَهَنَّمَ» — "Those who will be mustered on their faces toward hell" is a metonymy for the faithless who defamed the Quran and described the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with what they described; and metonymy is more eloquent than explicit statement.

The meaning is that these critics of the Quran who describe you are the ones in a worse plight and further astray from the way, not you. The speech is built upon "reversal of the attribute" (قصر القلب), and the words "worse" (شر) and "further astray" (أضل) are stripped of the meaning of comparison or are used for sarcasm and the like.

He used the metonymy of them being "mustered on their faces toward hell," which is a description of those whom Allah has led astray from among the obstinate deniers of the Return (المعاد), as He, the Exalted, said: «وَمَنْ يَهْدِ اللهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِ وَمَنْ يُضْلِلْ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُمْ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِهِ وَنَحْشُرُهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عَلى وُجُوهِهِمْ عُمْياً وَبُكْماً وَصُمًّا مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ كُلَّما خَبَتْ زِدْناهُمْ سَعِيراً ذلِكَ جَزاؤُهُمْ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِنا» — "Whomever Allah guides is rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—for them you will never find any guardians besides Him. On the Day of Resurrection We shall muster them on their faces, blind, dumb, and deaf. Their abode shall be hell. Whenever it subsides, We shall intensify the blaze for them. That is their punishment because they disbelieved in Our signs..." (17:97-98).

In this metonymy, in addition to it being more eloquent, there is a threat to them of the worst place and painful punishment. Also, it is in the sense of an argument against their straying, for there is no straying further than for a person to walk on his face while he does not perceive what is in front of him. This straying that is in their being mustered on their faces toward hell is a representation of the straying they had in the world. It is as if it were said: "Indeed, these are the ones who are astray, for they are mustered on their faces," and none is afflicted with that except one who was astray in the world.

Their statements have differed regarding the aspect of the verse's connection to what precedes it. Some remained silent about it. It was mentioned in Majma' al-Bayan that they said to Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believers: "They are the worst of Allah's creation," so Allah, the Exalted, said: «أُوْلئِكَ شَرٌّ مَكاناً وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلاً» — "they are in a worse plight and further astray from the way." Some mentioned that it is connected to His saying a few verses prior: «أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلاً» — "The inhabitants of paradise on that day will have a better abode and a finer resting place." (25:24). You have already recognized what the context suggests.

They also differed regarding the meaning of their being "mustered on their faces." It was said: it is according to its literal meaning, which is moving while being prostrate. It was said: it is being dragged.

It was said: it is moving from place to place upside down, which is the opposite of walking upright; however, it was noted that it would have been more appropriate then to express it as being mustered "on their heads" rather than "on their faces." Allah, the Exalted, said in another place—which is like a description of what happens after this mustering: «يَوْمَ يُسْحَبُونَ فِي النَّارِ عَلى وُجُوهِهِمْ» — "The day they are dragged on their faces into the Fire..." (54:48).

It was said: the meaning is extreme humiliation, debasement, and disgrace metaphorically. The response to this is that metaphor is only resorted to when it is not possible to carry the word according to its literal truth.

It was said: it is from the saying of the Arabs: "So-and-so went on his face" when he does not know where he went. The response is that this refers back to ignorance of the place to which they are mustered, and that does not suit the restriction of the mustering in the verse by His saying: «إِلى جَهَنَّمَ» — "toward hell."

It was said: the speech is a metonymy or a representational metaphor (استعارة تمثيلية), and the meaning is that they are mustered while their hearts are attached to the lower things of the world and its ornaments, their faces being turned toward them. It was objected that there they are too preoccupied to turn toward the world and for their hearts to be attached to it; perhaps the meaning is the persistence of the effects of that in them and upon them.

The response to this is that the requirement of the verses on the "embodiment of deeds" (تجسم الأعمال) is that the punishment is a representation of the attachment to the world and the turning toward it; so in reality, they have no preoccupation on that day except that.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ وَجَعَلْنا مَعَهُ أَخاهُ هارُونَ وَزِيراً» — "Certainly We gave Moses the Book and We appointed his brother Aaron with him as a vizier." (25:35). This is a testimony to the messengership of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the revelation of the Book to him, in the face of the disbelievers' rejection of him and his Book, by citing the messengership of Moses, the granting of the Book to him, and the association of Aaron in his affair, in order to transition to the mention of the punishment of the people of Pharaoh and their destruction. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «فَقُلْنَا اذْهَبا إِلَى الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا فَدَمَّرْناهُمْ تَدْمِيراً» — "Then We said, ‘Go, both of you, to the people who have denied Our signs.’ Then We destroyed them with a total destruction." (25:36). It is said in Majma' al-Bayan: Destruction (التدمير) is annihilation for a wondrous cause, and from it comes exemplary punishment (التنكيل); it is said: "He destroyed (دمر) so-and-so" if he attacked him with something detestable. End quote.

The intended "signs" (الآيات) are the signs in the horizons and within the souls (آيات الآفاق والأنفس) that indicate monotheism, which they denied. Abu al-Sa'ud mentioned in his commentary that the signs are the nine detailed miracles that appeared at the hands of Moses (peace be upon him). He argued that the people were not described to the two of them with this description at the time of their being sent to them, because the denial of the signs necessarily follows their manifestation, which follows their going, which follows the command to do so. Rather, they were described as such during the narration to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to explain the cause of their deserving the destruction narrated after it—meaning: "So they both went to them and showed them all Our signs, but they denied them with a persistent denial, so We destroyed them." End quote. This is good if it were certain that the signs must be restricted to the signs of Moses (peace be upon him).

The aspect of the connection of the two verses to what precedes them is the threat to the critics of the Prophet's Book and messengership by analogizing the matter to the matter of Moses, where Allah gave him the Book and sent him with his brother to the people of Pharaoh, but they denied him, so He destroyed them with a total destruction.

For this subtle point, the mention of giving the Book was placed before the mention of sending them to the people and their destruction, even though the Torah was only revealed after the drowning of Pharaoh and his soldiers. Thus, the purpose of the story was only to point to the giving of the Book and the messengership to Moses and the destruction of the people due to denial.

It was said: the two verses are connected to His saying, the Exalted, previously: «وَكَفى بِرَبِّكَ هادِياً وَنَصِيراً» — "Your Lord suffices as a guide and helper." (25:31). This is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ لَمَّا كَذَّبُوا الرُّسُلَ أَغْرَقْناهُمْ وَجَعَلْناهُمْ لِلنَّاسِ آيَةً وَأَعْتَدْنا لِلظَّالِمِينَ عَذاباً أَلِيماً» — "And the people of Noah, when they impugned the apostles, We drowned them, and We made them a sign for mankind, and We have prepared a painful punishment for the wrongdoers." (25:37). It is apparent that His saying: «قَوْمَ نُوحٍ» — "the people of Noah" is in the accusative case due to an omitted verb indicated by His saying: «أَغْرَقْناهُمْ» — "We drowned them."

The meaning of their "impugning the apostles" is their impugning Noah, for the denial of one of the messengers of Allah is a denial of all of them because of their agreement on the word of truth. Furthermore, these nations were polytheistic peoples who denied prophethood and rejected messengership from the very start.

His saying: «وَجَعَلْناهُمْ لِلنَّاسِ آيَةً» — "and We made them a sign for mankind" means for those who remained after them of their descendants. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَعاداً وَثَمُودَ وَأَصْحابَ الرَّسِّ وَقُرُوناً بَيْنَ ذلِكَ كَثِيراً» — "And [the peoples of] ‘Ad and Thamud, and the inhabitants of Rass, and many generations between them." (25:38). It is said in Majma' al-Bayan: al-Rass (الرس) is the well that has not been lined with stones. They mentioned that they were a people after Thamud who settled by a well; Allah sent a messenger to them, but they denied him, so Allah destroyed them. It was said it is the name of a river on whose bank they lived, and there are narrations from the Shi'a that support this.

His saying: «وَعاداً» etc., is coordinated with «قَوْمَ نُوحٍ», and the estimation is: "And We destroyed or We annihilated 'Ad and Thamud and the inhabitants of Rass..." etc.

His saying: «وَقُرُوناً بَيْنَ ذلِكَ كَثِيراً» — "and many generations between them." A "generation" (القرن) refers to the people of a single era, and sometimes it is applied to the era itself. The demonstrative "that" (ذلك) refers to the peoples mentioned, the first of whom were the people of Noah and the last of whom were the inhabitants of Rass or the people of Pharaoh. The meaning is: "And We destroyed or We annihilated 'Ad—who were the people of Hud—and Thamud—who were the people of Salih—and the inhabitants of Rass, and many generations interspersed between these whom We have mentioned, namely the people of Noah and those after them."

His saying, the Exalted: «وَكُلًّا ضَرَبْنا لَهُ الْأَمْثالَ وَكُلًّا تَبَّرْنا تَتْبِيراً» — "For each We struck parables, and each We destroyed with a total destruction." (25:39). "Each" (كلا) is in the accusative case due to a verb indicated by His saying: «ضَرَبْنا لَهُ الْأَمْثالَ» — "We struck parables for him", for the striking of parables is in the sense of reminding, admonishing, and warning. Total destruction (التتبير) is pulverization. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَقَدْ أَتَوْا عَلَى الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي أُمْطِرَتْ مَطَرَ السَّوْءِ أَفَلَمْ يَكُونُوا يَرَوْنَها بَلْ كانُوا لا يَرْجُونَ نُشُوراً» — "Certainly they have passed by the town that was rained upon by an evil rain. Have they not seen it? Rather, they did not expect any resurrection." (25:40). This town is the town of the people of Lot; Allah rained upon them stones of baked clay (سجيل). The detail of their stories has already passed in the previous chapters.

His saying: «أَفَلَمْ يَكُونُوا يَرَوْنَها» — "Have they not seen it?" is a reproachful interrogation, for the town was on the route of the people of Hijaz to the Levant.

His saying: «بَلْ كانُوا لا يَرْجُونَ نُشُوراً» — "Rather, they did not expect any resurrection" means they did not fear a return, or they were despairing of the return. This is like His saying, the Exalted, previously: «بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالسَّاعَةِ» — "Rather, they deny the Hour" (25:11). What is intended by it is that the fundamental source for their denial of the Book and the messengership, their failure to be admonished by these healing exhortations, and their failure to take heed from what others take heed of, is that they are deniers of the Return (المعاد); thus, no call succeeds with them, and no wisdom or admonition enters their hearts.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Uyun, with its chain of narration from Abu al-Salt al-Harawi from Imam al-Rida from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon them): a long hadith in which he mentions the story of the inhabitants of Rass (أصحاب الرس), the summary of which is: "They were a people who worshipped a pine tree—called Shah-Darakht—which Yafith son of Noah had planted after the Flood—on the edge of a spring called Rowshan-Ab. They had twelve flourishing villages on the bank of a river—called al-Rass—named with the names: Aban, Adhar, Day, Bahman, Isfandar, Farvardin, Ordibehesht, Khordad, Mordad, Tir, Mehr, Shahrivar; and from these the Persians derived the names of their months.

They had planted in each of those villages a seed from the fruit of that pine tree. They channeled a river to it from the spring that was by the pine tree, and they forbade the drinking of its water for themselves and their cattle—and whoever drank from it, they would kill him, saying: 'It is the life of the gods, so it is not appropriate for anyone to diminish its life.'

They had designated in each month of the year a day in each village as a festival—in which they would go out to the pine tree that was outside the village—offering sacrifices to it and slaughtering animals—then they would burn them in a fire they had kindled, and they would prostrate to the tree—when its smoke rose and spread in the sky—and they would weep and supplicate, and the devil would speak to them from the tree.

This was their habit in the villages—until it was the day of the festival of their greatest village—in which their king resided and its name was Isfandar—the people of all the villages gathered there and celebrated for twelve days, and they brought the most they could—of sacrifices and worship for the tree, and Iblis spoke to them—promising them and giving them hopes more than what the devils did—in the other festivals from the other trees.

When their disbelief in Allah and worship of the tree lasted long—Allah sent to them a messenger from the Children of Israel from the descendants of Judah—so he called them to the worship of Allah and the abandonment of polytheism for a period, but they did not believe—so he supplicated against the tree and it withered. When they saw that, it grieved them—so some of them said: 'This man has bewitched our gods,' and others said: 'Our gods have become angry with us because of that—when they saw this man calling us to disbelieve in them, and we left him to his own devices without becoming angry at him for our gods.'

So their opinions gathered upon killing him, so they dug a deep well—and threw him into it and sealed its top—and they did not cease to be over it, hearing his moaning until he died—so Allah followed them with a severe punishment that annihilated them to the last man."

In Nahj al-Balagha, he (peace be upon him) said: أين أصحاب مدائن الرس الذين قتلوا النبيين ـ وأطفئوا سنن المرسلين وأحيوا سنن الجبارين — "Where are the inhabitants of the cities of al-Rass who killed the prophets, extinguished the traditions of the messengers, and revived the traditions of the tyrants?"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Abi Hamzah, Hisham, and Hafs from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): that some women entered upon him, and one of them asked him about lesbianism (السحق)—so he said: "Its punishment is the punishment of the adulterer"—so the woman said: "Did Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, mention it in the Quran?" He said: "Yes." She said: "And where is it?" He said: "They are [the women of] al-Rass."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in Dhamm al-Malahi, al-Bayhaqi, and Ibn 'Asakir narrated from Ja'far ibn Muhammad ibn 'Ali: that two women asked him: "Do you find the sexual act of a woman with a woman forbidden in the Book of Allah?" He said: "Yes, they are those who were in the time of Tubba', and they are the female companions of al-Rass, and every river and well is a rass."

He said: "A garment of fire will be cut for them, and a coat of mail of fire, and a belt of fire, and a crown of fire, and two shoes of fire, and above that a thick, dry, coarse, stinking cloth of fire." Ja'far said: "Teach this to your women."

I say: al-Qummi narrated from his father from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from Jamil from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) something to its meaning.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from Hafs ibn Ghiyath from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: «وَكُلًّا تَبَّرْنا تَتْبِيراً» — meaning "We broke them with a total breaking." He said: "It is a word in Nabataean."

In it, and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "As for the town that was rained upon by an evil rain—it is Sodom, the town of the people of Lot—Allah rained upon them stones of sijjil, meaning of clay."

Ayahs

وَإِذَا رَأَوْكَ إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى بَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ رَسُولًا (٤١)
When they see you they just take you in derision: ‘Is this the one whom Allah has sent as an apostle!? (41)
إِن كَادَ لَيُضِلُّنَا عَنْ ءَالِهَتِنَا لَوْلَآ أَن صَبَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ حِينَ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْعَذَابَ مَنْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا (٤٢)
Indeed he was about to lead us astray from our gods, had we not stood firm by them.’ Soon they will know, when they sight the punishment, who is further astray from the [right] way. (42)
أَرَءَيْتَ مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَـٰهَهُۥ هَوَىٰهُ أَفَأَنتَ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِ وَكِيلًا (٤٣)
Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god? Is it your duty to watch over him? (43)
أَمْ تَحْسَبُ أَنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ أَوْ يَعْقِلُونَ ۚ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا كَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۖ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا (٤٤)
Do you suppose that most of them listen or exercise their reason? They are just like cattle; indeed, they are further astray from the way. (44)
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ ٱلظِّلَّ وَلَوْ شَآءَ لَجَعَلَهُۥ سَاكِنًا ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا ٱلشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلًا (٤٥)
Have you not regarded how your Lord spreads the twilight? (Had He wished He would have made it stand still.) Then We made the sun a beacon for it. (45)
ثُمَّ قَبَضْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْنَا قَبْضًا يَسِيرًا (٤٦)
Then We retract it toward Ourselves, with a gentle retracting. (46)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِبَاسًا وَٱلنَّوْمَ سُبَاتًا وَجَعَلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ نُشُورًا (٤٧)
It is He who made for you the night as a covering and sleep for rest and He made the day a recall to life. (47)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً طَهُورًا (٤٨)
And it is He who sends the winds as harbingers of His mercy, and We send down from the sky purifying water, (48)
لِّنُحْـِۧىَ بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا وَنُسْقِيَهُۥ مِمَّا خَلَقْنَآ أَنْعَـٰمًا وَأَنَاسِىَّ كَثِيرًا (٤٩)
with which We revive a dead country and provide water to many of the cattle and humans We have created. (49)
وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَـٰهُ بَيْنَهُمْ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا۟ فَأَبَىٰٓ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُورًا (٥٠)
