Certainly We distribute it among them so that they may take admonition. But most people are only intent on ingratitude. (50)
وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَبَعَثْنَا فِى كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ نَّذِيرًا (٥١)
Had We wished, We would have sent a warner to every town. (51)
فَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَجَـٰهِدْهُم بِهِۦ جِهَادًا كَبِيرًا (٥٢)
So do not obey the faithless, but wage a great jihad against them with it. (52)
۞ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى مَرَجَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ هَـٰذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ وَهَـٰذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخًا وَحِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا (٥٣)
It is He who merged the two seas: this one sweet and agreeable, and that one briny and bitter, and between the two He set a barrier and a forbidding hindrance. (53)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ بَشَرًا فَجَعَلَهُۥ نَسَبًا وَصِهْرًا ۗ وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيرًا (٥٤)
It is He who created the human being from water, then invested him with ties of blood and marriage, and your Lord is all-powerful. (54)
وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُهُمْ وَلَا يَضُرُّهُمْ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱلْكَافِرُ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ ظَهِيرًا (٥٥)
They worship besides Allah that which neither brings them any benefit nor causes them any harm, and the faithless one is ever an abettor against his Lord. (55)
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا (٥٦)
We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and as a warner. (56)
قُلْ مَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَن شَآءَ أَن يَتَّخِذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا (٥٧)
Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, except that anyone who wishes should take the way to his Lord.’ (57)
وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱلْحَىِّ ٱلَّذِى لَا يَمُوتُ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِهِۦ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِهِۦ بِذُنُوبِ عِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًا (٥٨)
Put your trust in the Living One who does not die, and celebrate His praise. He suffices as one all-aware of the sins of His servants. (58)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۚ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ فَسْـَٔلْ بِهِۦ خَبِيرًا (٥٩)
He, who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days, and then settled on the Throne, the All-beneficent; so ask someone who is well aware about Him. (59)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ قَالُوا۟ وَمَا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ أَنَسْجُدُ لِمَا تَأْمُرُنَا وَزَادَهُمْ نُفُورًا ۩ (٦٠)
When they are told: ‘Prostrate yourselves before the All-beneficent,’ they say, ‘What is ‘‘the All-beneficent’’? Shall we prostrate ourselves before whatever you bid us?’ And it increases their aversion. (60)
تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ بُرُوجًا وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا سِرَٰجًا وَقَمَرًا مُّنِيرًا (٦١)
Blessed is He who appointed houses in the sky and set in it a lamp and a shining moon. (61)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ خِلْفَةً لِّمَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يَذَّكَّرَ أَوْ أَرَادَ شُكُورًا (٦٢)
It is He who made the night and the day alternate for someone who desires to take admonition, or desires to give thanks. (62)

(Commentary)

The verses mention some of the attributes of those disbelievers (الكفار) who impugn the Book and the Message and deny monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and final judgment of all souls. These attributes correspond to the nature of their objections and suggestions, such as their mocking of the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), their following of caprice (الهوى), their worship of that which neither benefits nor harms them, and their arrogance (استكبارهم) in refusing to prostrate to Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: وإِذا رَأَوْكَ إِنْ يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُواً أَهذَا الَّذِي بَعَثَ اللهُ رَسُولاً — "When they see you, they only take you in derision: ‘Is this the one whom Allah has sent as an apostle?’" (25:41). The plural pronoun refers to those who disbelieve mentioned previously. Derision (الهزؤ) means mockery and ridicule; here, the verbal noun carries the meaning of the object. The meaning is: When those who disbelieve see you, they take you as nothing but an object of mockery.

His saying: أَهذَا الَّذِي بَعَثَ اللهُ رَسُولاً — "Is this the one whom Allah has sent as an apostle?" is an explanation of their mockery; that is, they say this to mock you.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ كادَ لَيُضِلُّنا عَنْ آلِهَتِنا لَوْ لا أَنْ صَبَرْنا عَلَيْها — "‘He had almost led us astray from our gods, had we not stood by them.’" (25:42). The particle إِنْ is the lightened form of the heavy (مخففة من الثقيلة). Leading astray (الإضلال) here seems to imply the meaning of "turning away," which is why it is followed by the preposition "from" (عن). The apodosis of "had not" (لو لا) is omitted, indicated by what precedes it. The meaning is that he nearly turned us away from our gods, leading us astray, had we not remained steadfast upon our gods—that is, upon their worship—he would have turned us away from them.

His saying: وَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ حِينَ يَرَوْنَ الْعَذابَ مَنْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلاً — "But soon they will know, when they see the punishment, who is further astray from the way." is a threat and a warning from Him, the Exalted, to them. It is an alert that they are heedless of what awaits them: the witnessing of the punishment and the certainty of their error and deviance.

His saying, the Exalted: أَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ أَفَأَنْتَ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِ وَكِيلاً — "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god? Is it your duty to be his guardian?" (25:43). Caprice (الهوى) — the soul's inclination toward lust and passion without the moderation of intellect — is what is meant here. Taking caprice as a god means obeying and following it instead of Allah. Allah, the Glorified, has frequently censured the following of caprice in His speech and has considered the obedience to a thing as worship of it, as in His saying: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me.’" (36:60-61).

His saying: أَفَأَنْتَ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِ وَكِيلاً — "Is it your duty to be his guardian?" is a rhetorical question of denial (استفهام إنكاري). It means: You are not a guardian (وكيلاً) over him, responsible for his soul and his affairs such that you could guide him to the path of rectitude (سبيل الرشد). This is not within your power, for Allah has led him astray and severed from him the means of guidance (الهداية) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path. Similar in meaning is His saying: إِنَّكَ لا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ — "You cannot guide whomever you wish" (28:56), and His saying: وَما أَنْتَ بِمُسْمِعٍ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ — "and you cannot make those who are in the graves hear" (35:22). This verse serves as a summary of the detail found in His saying: أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ وَخَتَمَ عَلى سَمْعِهِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَجَعَلَ عَلى بَصَرِهِ غِشاوَةً فَمَنْ يهديهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ اللهِ — "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly, and set a seal upon his hearing and his heart, and put a blindfold on his sight? So who will guide him after Allah?" (45:23).

It appears from the preceding meaning that His saying: اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ — "taken his desire to be his god" follows its natural syntax. That is, "taken" (اتخذ) is a transitive verb taking two objects: "his god" (إلهه) is the first object and "his desire" (هواه) is the second. This is what suits the context, as the discourse concerns the polytheism (شرك) of the polytheists and their turning away from the worship of Allah to the worship of idols, and their turning from obedience to the Truth—which is obedience to Allah—to obedience to caprice, which beautifies polytheism for them. These people admit they have a god who is obeyed, and they are correct in that principle; however, they believe this obeyed one is caprice, so they take it as the obeyed one instead of taking the Truth as the obeyed one. Thus, they have placed caprice in the position of the Truth, not that they have placed the "obeyed one" in the position of something else. Understand this.

From here, the weakness in the view of several exegetes becomes apparent, who claim that "his desire" (هواه) is the first object of "taken" (اتخذ) and "his god" (إلهه) is the second object placed forward. They argue it was placed forward for emphasis, as it is the pivot of the wonder expressed in His saying: أَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ — "Have you seen him who has taken...", as some have said. Others say it was placed forward for restriction (الحصر). They have long discussions on this which we have overlooked, and what we have mentioned is sufficient, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ تَحْسَبُ أَنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ أَوْ يَعْقِلُونَ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلاً — "Do you suppose that most of them listen or understand? They are just like cattle; rather they are further astray from the way." (25:44). The particle أَمْ is disjunctive (منقطعة). "Supposing" (الحسبان) means thinking or assuming. The plural pronouns refer to the relative pronoun in the previous verse in terms of meaning. The choice between "listening" and "understanding" is because the human means to the felicity of life is one of two things: either one is independent in intellection (التعقل), thus understanding the Truth and following it, or one refers to the word of one who understands and advises, following him if one is not independent in intellection. Thus, the path to rectitude is either hearing or intellect. The verse is similar in meaning to His saying: وَقالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ — "And they will say, ‘Had we listened or used our intellect, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze.’" (67:10).

The meaning is: Or do you think that most of them have the capacity to listen to the Truth to follow it, or the capacity to understand the Truth to follow it, such that you hope for their guidance and exert yourself in calling them?

His saying: إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا كَالْأَنْعامِ — "They are just like cattle" is an explanation of the preceding sentence, for it carries the meaning: Most of them do not listen and do not understand. Realize that they are nothing but like cattle and beasts in that they do not understand, nor do they hear anything but the sound, without the meaning.

His saying: بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلاً — "rather they are further astray from the way" means further astray than cattle. This is because cattle do not rush into what harms them, whereas these people prefer what harms them over what benefits them. Furthermore, if cattle are astray from the path of Truth, they were not equipped in their creation with that which would guide them to it, whereas these people are equipped yet have gone astray.

Some have used this verse to argue that cattle have no knowledge of their Lord. This is incorrect, for the verse does not negate the basic knowledge of Allah from them or from the disbelievers; rather, it negates from the disbelievers the following of the Truth to which the primordial human intellect (عقل الإنسان الفطري) guides, because it is veiled by the following of caprice. It likens them in this regard to cattle, which were not equipped with this type of perception.

As for the response of some that the speech is based on outward appearance, it is a claim that cannot be proven by demonstration.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ الظِّلَّ وَلَوْ شاءَ لَجَعَلَهُ ساكِناً ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا الشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلاً ثُمَّ قَبَضْناهُ إِلَيْنا قَبْضاً يَسِيراً — "Have you not regarded your Lord, how He extends the shadow? Had He wished, He would have made it still. Then We made the sun a guide for it. Then We draw it in toward Ourselves gently." (25:45-46). These two verses and the nine verses following them serve as an analogy (التنظير) for what was contained in the previous two verses—indeed, the previous four verses—regarding how Allah, the Glorified, established the Messenger's mission to guide people to the path of rectitude and save them from error. Some of them, whom Allah wills, are guided by it; as for others who have taken their caprice as their god and thus neither hear nor understand, it is not within anyone's power to guide them after Allah.

They explain that this is not something unprecedented from Allah, the Glorified; for in the wonders of His handiwork and the clear signs (بينات آياته) of His power, there are parallels to this. His acts are consistent, and He is upon a straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah. This is like the extending of the shadow and making the sun a guide over it to abrogate it; and like making the night a garment, sleep a rest, and the day a resurrection; and like sending the winds as heralds, sending down rain, reviving the dead land, and quenching the thirst of cattle and many humans with it.

Furthermore, the likeness of the believer and the disbeliever in the guidance of the former and the error of the latter—while they are all servants of Allah living on one earth—is but like the two waters: the sweet and thirsty-quenching, and the salty and bitter. Allah has let them loose to meet, yet He has placed between them an intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — and a forbidden barrier. And like the water from which Allah created a human, then made him into relations of lineage and marriage, whereby offspring differed, and your Lord is All-Powerful.

This is what reflection (التدبر) on the contents of the verses and the specifics of their arrangement leads to, and through it, the connection to what preceded becomes clear. As for what some have mentioned—that the verses are intended to explain some proofs of monotheism following the mention of the ignorance and error of those who turn away—the context does not support this, and we shall clarify this further.

His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ الظِّلَّ وَلَوْ شاءَ لَجَعَلَهُ ساكِناً — "Have you not regarded your Lord, how He extends the shadow? Had He wished, He would have made it still" is an analogy—as previously indicated—for the pervasiveness of ignorance and error among people and His removal of that through the Message and the true call as He wills. This necessitates that the "extending of the shadow" refers to the lengthening of the shadow that occurs after the sun passes its zenith (الزوال), extending gradually from west to east as the sun approaches the horizon, until it reaches its maximum extension at sunset, which is the night. In all its states, it is moving, and had Allah willed, He would have made it still.

His saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا الشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلاً — "Then We made the sun a guide for it." The sun is the guide in the sense that it indicates through its light that there is a shadow, and through its gradual spreading, it indicates the gradual extension of the shadow. Were it not for the sun, the existence of the shadow would not be noticed. The general reason for a human to distinguish between meanings is the transition of different states—from loss to finding. When one loses something they used to find, they notice its existence; and when they find what they used to lack, they notice its absence. As for a constant matter whose state does not change, there is no way to conceptualize it through noticing.

His saying: ثُمَّ قَبَضْناهُ إِلَيْنا قَبْضاً يَسِيراً — "Then We draw it in toward Ourselves gently" means We removed the shadow by the sun's radiance and its gradual rising until it is completely abrogated. Expressing removal and abrogation as "drawing in" (القبض), attributing it to Himself ("toward Ourselves"), and describing it as "gentle" (يسيراً) indicates the perfection of Divine power—that no act is difficult for Him—and that the loss of things after their existence is not through annihilation or nullification, but through returning to Him, the Exalted.

The preceding interpretation of "extending the shadow" as the lengthening of the shade after the sun's zenith, although not mentioned by the exegetes, is the only one the context—as we have pointed out—supports. Other views mentioned by exegetes include: that the extended shadow is the period between dawn and sunrise; or between sunset and sunrise; or the shadow cast by a dense object like a mountain, building, or tree against the sun after its rising; or—the most absurd of views—that it was the shadow on the day Allah created the heaven and made it like a dome, then spread the earth beneath it, so it cast its shadow upon it.

In the verse, namely His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى رَبِّكَ — "Have you not regarded your Lord...", there is a shift (التفات) from the first-person plural context in the previous verses to the third person. The subtle point (النكتة) is that the purpose of this verse and those following it is to explain that the matter of guidance belongs to Allah, the Glorified, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has no part in it; and that He, the Exalted, does not wish for their guidance. The Message and the true call, in their opposition to the error spread over the people of deviance and their abrogation of it, are part of the general Divine Sunnah (السنة العامة الإلهية) in spreading mercy over His creation, similar to the sun's rising over the earth and its abrogation of the extended shadow. It is evident that the address containing this reality should be specific to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), especially regarding the negation of his power to guide. As for the disbelievers who have taken their caprice as their god and who neither hear nor understand, they have no share in it.

In His saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا الشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلاً ثُمَّ قَبَضْناهُ إِلَيْنا — "Then We made the sun a guide for it. Then We draw it in toward Ourselves...", there is a return to the previous first-person plural context. In this, there is an expression of majesty and an indication of greatness that is not hidden.

The discussion regarding His coming saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ — "It is He who made the night for you...", and His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ — "It is He who sends the winds...", and His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ — "It is He who let loose the two seas...", and His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْماءِ بَشَراً — "It is He who created the human from water...", is like the discussion regarding His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى رَبِّكَ — "Have you not regarded your Lord...". And the discussion regarding His saying: وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً — "and We sent down water from the sky...", and His saying: وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْناهُ بَيْنَهُمْ — "We have certainly distributed it among them...", and His saying: وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَبَعَثْنا — "Had We wished, We would have sent...", is like the discussion regarding His saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا الشَّمْسَ — "Then We made the sun...".

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ لِباساً وَالنَّوْمَ سُباتاً وَجَعَلَ النَّهارَ نُشُوراً — "It is He who made the night for you as a garment, and sleep for a rest, and He made the day a time for rising." (25:47). The night being a "garment" (لباساً) is because it covers the human by the enshrouding of darkness, just as a garment covers its wearer.

His saying: وَالنَّوْمَ سُباتاً — "and sleep for a rest" means a cessation of work. His saying: وَجَعَلَ النَّهارَ نُشُوراً — "and He made the day a time for rising" means He made within it the spreading out and the seeking of sustenance, as mentioned by al-Raghib regarding the meaning of these two words.

The state of His covering people with the garment of night and their cessation of work and movement through it, then their rising for work and effort by the appearance of the day and the spreading of light, is like the state of extending the shadow, then making the sun a guide over it and drawing the shadow back to Himself through it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ بُشْراً بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً طَهُوراً — "And it is He who sends the winds as heralds of His mercy, and We send down from the sky purifying water," (25:48). The word بُشْراً (with damma then sukun) is a contraction of بُشُراً (with two dammas), the plural of بشور, meaning "bringer of glad tidings." That is, He is the one who sends the winds as heralds before His mercy, which is the rain.

His saying: وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً طَهُوراً — "and We send down from the sky purifying water" means from the direction of height, which is the atmosphere of the earth, a water that is "purifying" (طهوراً)—that is, extreme in its purity. It is pure in itself and purifying for others; it removes filth and takes away impurities and ritual defilements. Thus, "purifying" (الطهور), as has been said, is an intensive form (صيغة مبالغة).

His saying, the Exalted: لِنُحْيِيَ بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتاً وَنُسْقِيَهُ مِمَّا خَلَقْنا أَنْعاماً وَأَناسِيَّ كَثِيراً — "that We may revive with it a dead town and give it to drink to many of the cattle and humans We have created." (25:49). The "town" (البلدة) is well-known. It is said that what is meant is "place," as in His saying: وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the permission of its Lord" (7:58). Therefore, it was described as "dead" (مَيْتاً) in the masculine form. A dead place is one with no vegetation, and its revival is its bringing forth of plants. الأناسي is the plural of إنسان (human). The meaning of the verse is clear.

The state of death encompassing the earth and the need for drink and irrigation for cattle and humans, then His sending down purifying water from the sky to revive a dead town and give drink to cattle and many humans of His creation, is like the state of extending the shadow, then the indication of it by the sun and its abrogation by it, as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْناهُ بَيْنَهُمْ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا فَأَبى أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُوراً — "We have certainly distributed it among them so that they may take admonition, but most people are only intent on ingratitude." (25:50). The apparent connection of the verse to what precedes it is that the pronoun in "distributed it" (صَرَّفْناهُ) refers to the water. Its distribution among them means turning it from one people to others at one time, and from others to them at another. Thus, it does not continue falling upon one people such that they perish, nor is it permanently cut off from a people such that they perish; rather, it circulates among them so that each receives his portion according to the wisdom (المصلحة). It is also said that "distribution" (التصريف) means moving it from place to place.

His saying: لِيَذَّكَّرُوا فَأَبى أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُوراً — "so that they may take admonition, but most people are only intent on ingratitude" is the reason for the distribution. That is: I swear, We have distributed the water by dividing it among them so that they might remember and thus be grateful, but most people refused and abstained from anything but ingratitude for the blessing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَبَعَثْنا فِي كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ نَذِيراً — "Had We wished, We would have sent a warner to every town." (25:51). That is: Had We willed to send a warner in every town to warn them and a messenger to convey Our messages, We would have done so. However, We sent you to all the towns as a warner and a messenger due to your great status with Us. This is how the verse has been interpreted, and the following verse is not without support for this. This meaning is more suitable for the way we have explained the connection of the verses.

Alternatively, it means: We are capable of sending a messenger to every town, but We chose you for a wisdom in your selection.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تُطِعِ الْكافِرِينَ وَجاهِدْهُمْ بِهِ جِهاداً كَبِيراً — "So do not obey the faithless, and strive against them with it, a magnificent striving." (25:52). This is derived from the meaning of the previous verse. The pronoun in "with it" (بِهِ) refers to the Quran, as evidenced by the context of the verses. Striving (المجاهدة) and struggle (الجهاد) mean exerting effort and power in repelling the enemy. Since it is "with the Quran," what is meant is reciting it to them, explaining its realities to them, and completing the proofs (إتمام حججه) against them.

The sum of the verse's content is: Since the likeness of the Divine Message in removing the veil of ignorance and heedlessness cast over the hearts of people—by manifesting the Truth to them and completing the proof against them—is like the sun in its indication of the extended shadow and its abrogation by Allah's command; and like the day in relation to the night and its rest; and like the rain in relation to the dead earth and the thirsty cattle and humans; and since We have sent you to be a warner for the people of the towns, then do not obey the disbelievers, for their obedience nullifies this general law established for guidance. Exert the utmost of your effort and capacity in conveying your message and completing your proof with the Quran, which contains the true call, and strive against them with it a magnificent striving.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ هذا عَذْبٌ فُراتٌ وَهذا مِلْحٌ أُجاجٌ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَهُما بَرْزَخاً وَحِجْراً مَحْجُوراً — "It is He who let loose the two seas: this one is sweet and thirsty-quenching, and this one is salty and bitter, and He has placed between them a barrier and a forbidden obstruction." (25:53). Letting loose (المرج) means mixing; from it comes "a confused matter" (أمر مريج), meaning mixed. "Sweet" (العذب) water is that which has a pleasant taste; "thirsty-quenching" (الفرات) is that which is intensely sweet. "Salty" (الملح) is water whose taste has changed. "Bitter" (الأجاج) is intensely salty. The "barrier" (البرزخ) is the limit separating two things. "A forbidden obstruction" (حجراً مَحْجُوراً) means it is strictly forbidden for one of the two waters to mix with the other.

His saying: وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَهُما — "and He has placed between them..." is a contextual clue that "letting loose the two seas" means sending the two waters side-by-side, not mixing in the sense of blending their parts with one another.

The speech is coordinated with that to which His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ — "It is He who sends the winds..." was coordinated. In it is an analogy for the matter of the Message in terms of its leading to the distinction of the believer from the disbeliever, despite both groups living on one earth, intermingled, yet they do not blend, as previously indicated at the beginning of the nine verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْماءِ بَشَراً فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَباً وَصِهْراً وَكانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيراً — "It is He who created the human from water, then He established for him lineage and marriage, and your Lord is All-powerful." (25:54). Marriage (الصهر), as reported from al-Khalil, refers to the son-in-law and the household of the woman. Thus, lineage (النسب) is the prohibition of marriage from the male side, and marriage (الصهر) is the prohibition from the female side—as has been said—and this is supported by the contrast between lineage and marriage.

It has been said that both lineage and marriage are by the estimation of a suppressed word, the meaning being: "He made him possessor of lineage and marriage." The pronoun refers to the human. What is meant by "water" is the sperm-drop (النطفة). It is also possible that what is meant is water in general, from which Allah created all living things, as He said: وَجَعَلْنا مِنَ الْماءِ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ حَيٍّ — "and We made every living thing from water" (21:30).

The meaning is: He is the one who created from the sperm-drop—which is a single water—a human, then divided him into two parts: one possessing lineage and one possessing marriage, meaning the male and the female. This is another analogy signifying what the previous verse signified: that it is for Allah, the Glorified, to preserve multiplicity within the very essence of unity, and separation within the very essence of union. Likewise, He preserves the difference of souls and opinions through faith (الإيمان) and disbelief (الكفر) despite the unity of human society, through what Allah sent of messengers to uncover the veil of error that would naturally enshroud it were it not for the true call.

His saying: وَكانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيراً — "and your Lord is All-powerful." In the addition of "Lord" to the second-person pronoun, there is a subtle point similar to what preceded in His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى رَبِّكَ — "Have you not regarded your Lord...".

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ ما لا يَنْفَعُهُمْ وَلا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَكانَ الْكافِرُ عَلى رَبِّهِ ظَهِيراً — "They worship besides Allah what neither benefits them nor harms them, and the faithless is ever a partisan [of Satan] against his Lord." (25:55). This is coordinated with His saying: وَإِذا رَأَوْكَ إِنْ يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُواً — "When they see you, they only take you in derision...". "Partisan" (الظهير) means a backer or helper, as has been said.

The meaning is: They worship—these disbelieving polytheists—besides Allah that which does not benefit them by bringing good if they worship it, nor harms them by bringing evil if they abandon its worship. The disbeliever is a helper to Satan against his Lord.

The fact that these worshipped ones—the idols—outwardly neither benefit nor harm does not contradict the fact that their worship is harmful. Negating harm from the idols themselves, as they are powerless over anything, does not necessitate negating harm from their worship, which is harmful and leads the human to inevitable wretchedness and eternal punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّراً وَنَذِيراً — "We did not send you but as a bearer of good news and as a warner." (25:56). That is: We have not given you in your message anything but the bearing of good news and warning, and you have no authority beyond that. So it is not your concern if they are defiant toward their Lord, backing His enemy against Him; for they cannot frustrate Allah, and they only plot against themselves. This is what the context provides.

Accordingly, His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّراً وَنَذِيراً — "We did not send you but as a bearer of good news and as a warner" is a section of speech similar to His saying: أَفَأَنْتَ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِ وَكِيلاً — "Is it your duty to be his guardian?" in the previous section.

From this, it becomes clear that the view of some—that the verse is a consolation from Him, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), meaning: "We did not send you but as a bearer of good news for the believers and a warner for the disbelievers, so do not grieve over their lack of faith"—is not sound.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, except that anyone who wishes should take the way to his Lord.’" (25:57). The pronoun in "for it" (عَلَيْهِ) refers to the Quran, insofar as its recitation to them conveys the Message, as He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ هذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً — "This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take the way to his Lord" (73:19, 76:29), and He said: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, nor am I one of the impostors. It is just a reminder for all the nations.’" (38:86-87).

His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً — "except that anyone who wishes should take the way to his Lord" is a disjunctive exception (استثناء منقطع) carrying the meaning of a conjunctive one. For it means: "except that he who wishes so should take a way to his Lord," similar to His saying: يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَلا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ — "the day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart" (26:88-89); that is, except the coming to Allah with a sound heart by one who comes with it.

In this, the subject—the one who takes the way—is placed in the position of his act—the taking of the way—as a gesture of gratitude to him. Thus, the speech considers their taking a way to Allah, the Glorified, by responding to the call, as a reward for himself. This hints at his ultimate independence from any monetary or social reward from them, and that he desires nothing from them beyond their response to the call and their following of the Truth—no wealth, no status, nor any supposed reward. So let them be at ease and not suspect him in his sincere advice.

He has made the taking of the way dependent on their will to indicate their complete freedom from his side (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). There is no compulsion or coercion, for he has no duty from his Lord beyond the bearing of good news and warning. He is not a guardian over them; rather, the matter belongs to Allah, who judges among them as He wills.

Thus, His saying: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, except that anyone who wishes should take...’", after establishing for His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he has nothing but the Message through bearing good news and warning, commands him to inform them that he has no desire in calling them except that they respond to him and take a way to their Lord, without any additional purpose of reward whatsoever. And that they have the choice in their affair without any compulsion or coercion; they and the call are as they wish: if they wish, let them believe, and if they wish, let them disbelieve.

This is what pertains to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), which is the conveying of the Message alone, without greed for reward, without imposing upon them by compulsion, and without taking revenge upon them by punishment. As for what is beyond that, it belongs to Allah; so let him refer it to Him and rely upon Him, as indicated in the following verse: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لا يَمُوتُ — "And put your trust in the Living One who does not die."

The majority of exegetes mentioned that the exception is disjunctive, the meaning being: "But whoever wishes to take a way to his Lord—such as by spending that which stands in place of a reward, like charity and spending in the way of Allah—let him do so." This is weak and has no evidence, neither from the wording of the sentence nor from the context.

Some said it is conjunctive and the speech has a suppressed word, the estimation being: "except the act of him who wishes to take a way to his Lord through faith and obedience according to what I call to." In this, their response to him is taken as a reward for himself, completely severing any suspicion of greed and putting them at ease. This view, in terms of meaning, returns to what we have presented, but it is distinguished by the estimation of a suppressed word, and estimation is contrary to the original rule.

Others said it is conjunctive with a suppressed word, the estimation being: "I do not ask you any reward for it except the reward of him who wishes..." etc.; that is, except the reward accruing to me from his faith, for the one who points to good is like the one who does it. The problem with this is that the requirement of this meaning would be to say: "except him who takes a way to his Lord," so there would be no need to make the taking dependent on the will, and the reward is only based on the action, not the will for it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لا يَمُوتُ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِهِ وَكَفى بِهِ بِذُنُوبِ عِبادِهِ خَبِيراً — "And put your trust in the Living One who does not die, and celebrate His praise. He suffices as one all-aware of His servants’ sins." (25:58). When He established for His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he has no part in their affair except the Message, and commanded him to inform them that he has no desire in his call except their response, and that they have the choice in their affair—to believe if they wish or disbelieve if they wish—He completed that by commanding him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to take Him, the Exalted, as a guardian (وكيلاً) in their affair. For He, the Exalted, is the guardian over them and over all things, and He is all-aware of His servants' sins.

His saying: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لا يَمُوتُ — "And put your trust in the Living One who does not die" means: Take Him as a guardian in their affair; He will judge among them as He wills and do with them what He intends, for He is the guardian over them and over all things. He turned from making the trust dependent on "Allah" to making it dependent on "the Living One who does not die" to provide the reason (التعليل); for the Living One who does not die is not escaped by anything that passes, so He is the one designated to be the guardian.

His saying: وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِهِ — "and celebrate His praise" means: Sanctify Him from incapacity, ignorance, and everything that does not befit the courtyard of His holiness, accompanying that with praise for Him for His beautiful attributes. If He grants them respite and draws them on gradually (استدرجهم) with His blessings, it is not out of incapacity that He did so, nor out of ignorance of their sins. And if He seizes them for their sins, it is due to a wisdom that necessitated it and a desert from them that called for it. So, glory be to Him and by His praise.

His saying: وَكَفى بِهِ بِذُنُوبِ عِبادِهِ خَبِيراً — "He suffices as one all-aware of His servants’ sins" is intended to indicate His monotheism in His acts and attributes. He is the Guardian who disposes of the affairs of His servants alone, and He is all-aware of their sins and the Judge among them alone, without need for anyone to assist Him in His knowledge or His judgment.

From here, it becomes clear that the following verse: الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ — "He who created the heavens and the earth..." is a completion of His saying: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لا يَمُوتُ — "And put your trust in the Living One who does not die...", because it contains His monotheism in His kingdom and His disposal, just as His saying: وَكَفى بِهِ — "He suffices..." contains His knowledge and awareness. Through life, kingdom, and knowledge together, the meaning of guardianship (الوكالة) is completed, as we shall indicate.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ الرَّحْمنُ فَسْئَلْ بِهِ خَبِيراً — "He who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days, and then settled on the Throne, the All-beneficent; so ask someone who is aware about Him." (25:59). The apparent context is that the relative pronoun is an attribute of His saying in the previous verse: الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لا يَمُوتُ — "the Living One who does not die". With this verse, the explanation in His saying: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لا يَمُوتُ — "And put your trust in the Living One who does not die" is completed. For guardianship, just as it depends on the life of the guardian, depends on knowledge—which He mentioned in His saying: وَكَفى بِهِ بِذُنُوبِ عِبادِهِ خَبِيراً — "He suffices as one all-aware of His servants’ sins"—and it depends on the authority to judge and dispose, which is what this verse contains through its mention of the creation of the heavens and the earth and the settling on the Throne.

The interpretation of the beginning of the verse has already been given in various places in the preceding Surahs. As for His saying: الرَّحْمنُ فَسْئَلْ بِهِ خَبِيراً — "the All-beneficent; so ask someone who is aware about Him," what the context provides and the arrangement indicates is that "the All-beneficent" (الرَّحْمنُ) is the predicate of a suppressed subject, the estimation being: "He is the All-beneficent." His saying: فَسْئَلْ — "so ask" is derived from it, and the fa is for derivation (التفريع). The preposition ba in His saying: بِهِ — "about Him" is for transitivity (التعدية), with the question implying the meaning of "concern." His saying: خَبِيراً — "someone who is aware" is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) of the pronoun.

The meaning is: He is the All-beneficent (الرَّحْمنُ) — the one who settled on the throne of the kingdom, and by whose mercy and emanation creation and command are sustained, and from whom everything begins and to whom everything returns — so ask Him about the reality of the matter, and He will inform you of it, for He is all-aware (خبير).

Thus, His saying: فَسْئَلْ بِهِ خَبِيراً — "so ask someone who is aware about Him" is a metaphor (كناية) for the fact that what He has informed of is the reality of the matter from which there is no deviation. This is like someone who, when asked about a matter, says: "Ask me and I will answer you that it is such and such." From this category is their saying: "You have stumbled upon the one who is aware."

Exegetes have many other views regarding His saying: الرَّحْمنُ فَسْئَلْ بِهِ خَبِيراً. It is said that "the All-beneficent" (الرَّحْمنُ) is in the nominative case for praise (القطع للمدح). It is said it is a subject whose predicate is His saying: فَسْئَلْ بِهِ. It is said it is a predicate whose subject is "He who" (الَّذِي) at the beginning of the verse. It is said it is an appositive (بدل) for the latent pronoun in "settled" (اسْتَوى).

Regarding فَسْئَلْ بِهِ, it is said it is the predicate for "the All-beneficent" as mentioned, and the fa is eloquent (فصيحة). It is said it is an independent sentence derived from what preceded, and the fa is for derivation; then the ba in بِهِ is for connection or carries the meaning of "about," and the pronoun refers to Him, the Exalted, or to the previously mentioned creation and settling.

It is said خَبِيراً is a circumstantial qualifier for the pronoun, which refers to Him, the Exalted; the meaning being: "Ask Allah while He is all-aware." It is said it is the object of "ask," and the ba means "about," the meaning being: "Ask an aware one about the All-beneficent or about the account of creation and settling." The "aware one" is said to be Allah, the Glorified; or Gabriel; or Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); or whoever has read the ancient heavenly books and learned of His attributes, His acts, and the manner of creation and origination; or anyone who has knowledge of these realities.

Most or all of these scattered views do not suit what the context of the noble verses provides, and there is no need to discuss them or delve into them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اسْجُدُوا لِلرَّحْمنِ قالُوا وَمَا الرَّحْمنُ أَنَسْجُدُ لِما تَأْمُرُنا وَزادَهُمْ نُفُوراً — "When they are told, ‘Prostrate to the All-beneficent,’ they say, ‘What is the All-beneficent? Shall we prostrate to whatever you bid us?’ And it increases their aversion." (25:60). This is another section regarding their evil treatment of the Messenger and his true call. It mentions their arrogance in refusing to prostrate to Allah, the Glorified, when they are called to it, and their aversion to it. The verse has a specific connection to what preceded it through the mention of "the All-beneficent" (الرَّحْمنُ), who was described in the previous verse as He was described; perhaps the definite article here is for reference (للعهد).

His saying: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اسْجُدُوا لِلرَّحْمنِ — "When they are told, ‘Prostrate to the All-beneficent,’" the pronoun refers to the disbelievers. The speaker is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as evidenced by their subsequent saying: أَنَسْجُدُ لِما تَأْمُرُنا — "Shall we prostrate to whatever you bid us?". His name was not mentioned so that their arrogance would be directed toward Allah, the Glorified, alone.

His saying: قالُوا وَمَا الرَّحْمنُ — "they say, ‘What is the All-beneficent?’" is a question from them about His identity and quiddity (مائيته), as an exaggeration in feigning ignorance of Him out of arrogance toward Allah. Were it not for that, they would have said: "Who is the All-beneficent?" (ومن الرحمن). This is like Pharaoh's saying to Moses when he called him to the Lord of the worlds: وَما رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ — "And what is the Lord of all the worlds?" (26:23), and Abraham's saying to his people: ما هذِهِ التَّماثِيلُ الَّتِي أَنْتُمْ لَها عاكِفُونَ — "What are these statues to which you are so devoted?" (21:52). The intention of the questioner in such a question is that he has no knowledge of the one asked about beyond his name, as in Hud's saying to his people: أَتُجادِلُونَنِي فِي أَسْماءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ — "Do you dispute with me about names which you and your fathers have coined?" (7:71).

His saying, narrating from them: أَنَسْجُدُ لِما تَأْمُرُنا — "Shall we prostrate to whatever you bid us?" In the repetition of the expression "whatever" (ما) referring to Him, the Exalted, there is an insistence on arrogance. Expressing his request for them to prostrate as a "command" (الأمر) is not without sarcasm and mockery.

His saying: وَزادَهُمْ نُفُوراً — "And it increases their aversion" is coordinated with the apodosis of "when" (إذا). The meaning is: When they were told to prostrate, they were arrogant, and that increased their aversion. The subject of "increased them" (زادَهُمْ) is a pronoun referring to the "saying" understood from the preceding speech.

The view of some—that the subject is a pronoun referring to the "prostration," based on what they narrated that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his companions prostrated and they moved away from them mockingly—is not sound. For the occurrence of an event does not affect the indication of the wording unless the wording addresses it. There is no mention of this story in the verse at all.

His saying, the Exalted: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ فِي السَّماءِ بُرُوجاً وَجَعَلَ فِيها سِراجاً وَقَمَراً مُنِيراً — "Blessed is He who appointed constellations in the sky and appointed in it a lantern and a luminous moon." (25:61). It appears that what is meant by "constellations" (البروج) are the stations of the sun and the moon in the sky, or the stars that are upon them, as previously mentioned in His saying: وَلَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا فِي السَّماءِ بُرُوجاً وَزَيَّنَّاها لِلنَّاظِرِينَ وَحَفِظْناها مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْطانٍ رَجِيمٍ — "Certainly We have appointed constellations in the sky and adorned them for the onlookers, and We have guarded them from every outcast Satan" (15:16-17). They were specifically mentioned in the verse to point to the guarding and pelting mentioned there.

What is meant by the "lantern" (السراج) is the sun, as evidenced by His saying: وَجَعَلَ الْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُوراً وَجَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ سِراجاً — "and appointed the moon in them as a light, and appointed the sun as a lantern" (71:16).

They have established the verse as an argument from the unity of the wondrous celestial and terrestrial management (التدبير) for the unity of the Manager; thus, worship must be directed to Him and one's face turned away from others.

Reflection on the connection of the two verses to what preceded them and the context of the verses does not support this. For the content of the previous verse—their arrogance toward the All-beneficent when commanded to prostrate to Him and their mockery of the Messenger—does not have a sufficient relationship with arguing for the unity of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) such that it should follow it. Rather, what is suitable for this meaning is the manifestation of might and independence, and that they cannot frustrate Allah by these acts of theirs, nor are they outside His kingdom and authority.

What reflection provides is that His saying: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ فِي السَّماءِ بُرُوجاً — "Blessed is He who appointed constellations in the sky..." is intended as an expression of might and independence, and that they cannot frustrate Him by their arrogance toward Allah and their mockery of the Messenger. Rather, these people are prevented from approaching the courtyard of His proximity and ascending to the heaven of His presence. Divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) is nonetheless illuminating for His people and His servants through what Allah, the Glorified, has lit of the light of His guidance, which is the light of the Message.

Based on this, Allah, the Glorified, has praised Himself by mentioning His being blessed (تباركه) for appointing the guarded constellations that pelt the devils with piercing flames in the physical sky, and appointing the illuminating sun and the luminous moon in it to light the physical world. He pointed by that to what corresponds to it in reality: the illumination of the human world with the light of guidance from the Message, so that His servants may see by it—just as He mentions their state after these verses—and the repelling of the allies of the devils from ascending to Him by what He has prepared for their repelling of guarded, pelting constellations.

This is what the context provides, and these verses and those before them were arranged according to this style of explanation, as previously indicated in the commentary on His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ الظِّلَ — "Have you not regarded your Lord, how He extends the shadow?". Thus, the esoteric interpretation (التأويل) we have mentioned is not in the sense of turning the verses away from their outward meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ خِلْفَةً لِمَنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يَذَّكَّرَ أَوْ أَرادَ شُكُوراً — "It is He who made the night and the day to follow each other for him who desires to take admonition or desires to give thanks." (25:62). Succession (الخلفة) is a thing that stands in place of another and vice versa. It is as if it is a noun of type (بناء نوع) intended to carry the meaning of an attribute. The night and day being a "succession" means that each of them succeeds the other. Restricting the succession with His saying: لِمَنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يَذَّكَّرَ أَوْ أَرادَ شُكُوراً — "for him who desires to take admonition or desires to give thanks" is to indicate the substitution of each for the other in remembrance and gratitude.

The contrast between "taking admonition" (التذكر) and "giving thanks" (الشكور) implies that what is meant by taking admonition is the return to what the human knows by his primordial nature (فطرته) of the proofs indicating the monotheism of his Lord and the attributes and names that befit Him, the Exalted; its goal is faith in Allah. By "giving thanks" is meant the word or deed that informs of praise for Him for the beauty of what He has bestowed; it applies to His worship and the righteous deeds (صالح العمل) that accompany it.

Accordingly, the verse is an expression of might or a bestowal of favor for His making the night and the day such that each succeeds its companion. Thus, whoever missed faith in Him in this period of time can make it up in the other period; and whoever was not granted success for worship or any righteous deed in one of them can perform it in the other.

This is what the verse signifies. It also has a connection to His saying in the previous verse: وَجَعَلَ فِيها سِراجاً وَقَمَراً مُنِيراً — "and appointed in it a lantern and a luminous moon." In it is an indication that Allah, the Glorified, although He repelled those arrogant ones from ascending to the courtyard of His proximity, did not prevent His servants from drawing near to Him and seeking light from His light. So He made a day with a rising sun and a night with a luminous moon, and they possess succession: whoever misses remembrance or gratitude in one of them can perform it in the other.

Some have interpreted "taking admonition" as the obligatory prayer and "giving thanks" as the supererogatory prayer. The verse is capable of being applied to that, even if it is not restricted to it.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ — "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god?": al-Tabarani narrated from Abu Umamah that he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "There is no god worshipped besides Allah under the canopy of heaven that is greater in the sight of Allah than a followed caprice."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ الظِّلَ — "Have you not regarded your Lord, how He extends the shadow?": He said: "The shadow is what is between the rising of the dawn and the rising of the sun."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْماءِ — "It is He who created from water..." the verse: Ibn Sirin said: "It was revealed concerning the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and Ali ibn Abi Talib—Ali married Fatimah—so he was his cousin and the husband of his daughter; thus he was lineage and marriage."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: وَكانَ الْكافِرُ عَلى رَبِّهِ ظَهِيراً — "and the faithless is ever a partisan against his Lord": "Meaning Abu al-Hakam—whom the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) named Abu Jahl ibn Hisham."

I say: The two narrations are more like instances of application (الجري والتطبيق).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, Blessed and Exalted is He: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ فِي السَّماءِ بُرُوجاً — "Blessed is He who appointed constellations in the sky": "The constellations are the stars. The constellations for spring and summer are: Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, and Virgo. The constellations for autumn and winter are: Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, and Pisces—and they are twelve constellations."

In al-Faqih, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "Whatever you miss during the night, make it up during the day. Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, said: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ خِلْفَةً لِمَنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يَذَّكَّرَ أَوْ أَرادَ شُكُوراً — 'It is He who made the night and the day to follow each other for him who desires to take admonition or desires to give thanks.' Meaning that a man makes up what he missed during the night during the day, and what he missed during the day during the night."

Ayahs

وَعِبَادُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ هَوْنًا وَإِذَا خَاطَبَهُمُ ٱلْجَـٰهِلُونَ قَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا (٦٣)
The servants of the All-beneficent are those who walk humbly on the earth, and when the ignorant address them, say, ‘Peace!’ (63)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَبِيتُونَ لِرَبِّهِمْ سُجَّدًا وَقِيَـٰمًا (٦٤)
Those who spend the night for their Lord, prostrating and standing [in worship]. (64)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا ٱصْرِفْ عَنَّا عَذَابَ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ إِنَّ عَذَابَهَا كَانَ غَرَامًا (٦٥)
Those who say, ‘Our Lord! Turn away from us the punishment of hell. Indeed its punishment is enduring. (65)
إِنَّهَا سَآءَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا (٦٦)
Indeed it is an evil station and abode.’ (66)
وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ لَمْ يُسْرِفُوا۟ وَلَمْ يَقْتُرُوا۟ وَكَانَ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ قَوَامًا (٦٧)
Those who are neither wasteful nor tightfisted when spending, but balanced between these [two extremes]. (67)
وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ وَلَا يَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا يَزْنُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثَامًا (٦٨)
Those who do not invoke another deity besides Allah, and do not kill a soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause, and do not commit fornication. (Whoever does that shall encounter its retribution, (68)
يُضَـٰعَفْ لَهُ ٱلْعَذَابُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِۦ مُهَانًا (٦٩)
the punishment being doubled for him on the Day of Resurrection. In it he will abide in humiliation forever, (69)
إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ ٱللَّهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ حَسَنَـٰتٍ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٧٠)
except those who repent, attain faith, and act righteously. For such, Allah will replace their misdeeds with good deeds, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (70)
وَمَن تَابَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَإِنَّهُۥ يَتُوبُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَتَابًا (٧١)
And whoever repents and acts righteously indeed turns to Allah with due penitence). (71)
وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَشْهَدُونَ ٱلزُّورَ وَإِذَا مَرُّوا۟ بِٱللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا۟ كِرَامًا (٧٢)
Those who do not give false testimony, and when they come upon frivolity, pass by with dignity. (72)
وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَمْ يَخِرُّوا۟ عَلَيْهَا صُمًّا وَعُمْيَانًا (٧٣)
Those who, when reminded of the signs of their Lord, do not turn a deaf ear and a blind eye to them. (73)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا هَبْ لَنَا مِنْ أَزْوَٰجِنَا وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِنَا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ وَٱجْعَلْنَا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِمَامًا (٧٤)
And those who say, ‘Our Lord! Give us joy and comfort in our spouses and offspring, and make us imams of the Godwary.’ (74)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُجْزَوْنَ ٱلْغُرْفَةَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ وَيُلَقَّوْنَ فِيهَا تَحِيَّةً وَسَلَـٰمًا (٧٥)
Those shall be rewarded with sublime abodes for their patience and steadfastness, and they shall be met there with greetings and ‘Peace,’ (75)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ حَسُنَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا (٧٦)
to abide in them [forever], an excellent station and abode. (76)
قُلْ مَا يَعْبَؤُا۟ بِكُمْ رَبِّى لَوْلَا دُعَآؤُكُمْ ۖ فَقَدْ كَذَّبْتُمْ فَسَوْفَ يَكُونُ لِزَامًۢا (٧٧)
Say, ‘Were it not for the sake of summoning you [to faith], what store my Lord would have set by you? But you impugned [me and my summons], so it will soon follow as a result.’ (77)

(Commentary)

The verses mention the virtuous traits of the believers (المؤمنين), which stand in contrast to the evil qualities of the disbelievers (الكفار) previously described. These traits are summarized by the fact that they supplicate to their Lord and believe in His Messenger and the Book revealed to him, as opposed to the disbelievers' denial of that and their turning away from it to follow their whims (الهوى). For this reason, the verses conclude with His saying: قُلْ ما يَعْبَؤُا بِكُمْ رَبِّي لَوْ لا دُعاؤُكُمْ فَقَدْ كَذَّبْتُمْ فَسَوْفَ يَكُونُ لِزاماً "Say, ‘My Lord would not care for you were it not for your prayer; but you have denied [the truth], so it will soon be [your] binding [punishment].’" (25:77), and with this, the Surah ends.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ الَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ هَوْناً وَإِذا خاطَبَهُمُ الْجاهِلُونَ قالُوا سَلاماً "The servants of the All-beneficent are those who walk humbly on the earth, and when the ignorant address them, they say, ‘Peace!’" (25:63). Since the previous verse mentioned their arrogance toward Allah, the Glorified, and their disdain for the Noble Name, the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), He contrasts it in this verse by mentioning the opposite quality in the believers. He calls them "servants" (عباداً) and attributes them to Himself, using the name "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن)—the very name from which the disbelievers used to deviate and recoil.

The verse describes them with two of their characteristics:

First: That which is contained in His saying: الَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ هَوْناً "those who walk humbly on the earth." Humility (الهون), according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is submissiveness (التذلل). In this context, it is most likely that "walking on the earth" is a metaphor (كناية) for their life in interacting and socializing with people. Thus, within themselves, they are submissive to their Lord and humble toward people, because they are the servants of Allah, neither arrogant toward Allah nor seeking superiority over others without right. As for submissiveness toward the enemies of Allah in pursuit of the illusory might they possess, they are far above such a thing. If "humility" (الهون) is taken to mean gentleness and leniency, then the meaning is that they walk without arrogance or haughtiness.

Second: That which is contained in His saying: وَإِذا خاطَبَهُمُ الْجاهِلُونَ قالُوا سَلاماً "and when the ignorant address them, they say, ‘Peace!’" That is, when the ignorant address them with speech arising from their ignorance—speech they dislike being addressed with or which is burdensome to them, as inferred from the verb being linked to the description—they respond to them with that which is free from vice (سالم من القول) and speak to them a peaceful word (قولاً سلاماً) devoid of vain talk (اللغو) and sin (الإثم). The Exalted said: لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً وَلا تَأْثِيماً إِلَّا قِيلاً سَلاماً سَلاماً "They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech, but only the word, ‘Peace! Peace!’" (56:25-26). This refers to their not meeting ignorance with ignorance.

It has been said that this is the description of their day when they spread out among the people, while the description of their night is what the following verse describes.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَبِيتُونَ لِرَبِّهِمْ سُجَّداً وَقِياماً "Those who spend the night for their Lord, prostrating and standing." (25:64). Spending the night (البيتوتة) is to experience the night, whether one sleeps or not. "For their Lord" (لِرَبِّهِمْ) is connected to His saying "prostrating" (سُجَّداً). "Prostrating" (السجد) and "standing" (القيام) are the plurals of "one who prostrates" (ساجد) and "one who stands" (قائم). The meaning is their worship (العبادة) of Him, the Exalted, by falling to the ground and standing on their legs, of which prayer (الصلاة) is a primary instance.

The meaning is: they are those who experience the night while prostrating therein to their Lord and standing, alternating between prostration and standing. It is possible that this refers to the night vigil (التهجد) with the supererogatory prayers of the night.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا اصْرِفْ عَنَّا عَذابَ جَهَنَّمَ إِنَّ عَذابَها كانَ غَراماً "Those who say, ‘Our Lord! Turn away from us the punishment of hell. Indeed its punishment is a lasting grievance." (25:65). Lasting grievance (الغرام) — that which afflicts a person of severity or calamity, becoming inseparable from him and never leaving him. The rest of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّها ساءَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقاماً "Indeed it is an evil abode and station.’" (25:66). The pronoun refers to Hell (جهنم). "Abode" (المستقر) and "station" (المقام) are nouns of place derived from stability (الاستقرار) and residency (الإقامة). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ إِذا أَنْفَقُوا لَمْ يُسْرِفُوا وَلَمْ يَقْتُرُوا وَكانَ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ قَواماً "Those who, when they spend, are neither wasteful nor tightfisted, and stay at a mean between these [two extremes]." (25:67). Spending (الإنفاق) is the bestowing of wealth and its expenditure to meet the needs of oneself or others. Extravagance (الإسراف) is exceeding the limit, and it only occurs on the side of excess; in spending, it is transgressing what is appropriate in the bestowing of wealth. Parsimony (القتر) is the reduction in spending, and it stands in contrast to extravagance, as mentioned by al-Raghib. The terms (القتر), (الإقتار), and (التقتير) all carry the same meaning.

The term (القوام) with a fatha on the qaf means the middle path or justice (العدل), while with a kasra (القوام) it means that by which a thing subsists. His saying "between these" (بَيْنَ ذلِكَ) is connected to "a mean" (قَواماً). The meaning is: their spending was a just middle between the aforementioned extravagance and parsimony. Thus, His saying وَكانَ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ قَواماً is an explicit statement of what is understood from His saying إِذا أَنْفَقُوا لَمْ يُسْرِفُوا وَلَمْ يَقْتُرُوا. The beginning of the verse negates the two extremes of excess (الإفراط) and deficiency (التفريط) in spending, while its end establishes the middle path.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ لا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ "Those who do not invoke another god besides Allah..." (25:68) to the end of the verse. This refers to polytheism (الشرك). The foundations of idolatry (الوثنية) do not permit the invocation or worship of Him, the Exalted, at all—neither alone nor alongside their deities. Rather, they mandate the invocation and worship of their deities so that they may bring them near to Allah in station and intercede for them with Him.

Thus, the meaning of their "invoking another god besides Allah" is either an allusion to the fact that He, the Exalted, is the God invoked by primordial nature (الفطرة) in every state, so invoking another is the invocation of another god alongside Him, even if Allah is not mentioned.

Or it means that He, the Exalted, is established in Himself whether another is invoked or not; thus, the intent of invoking another is the invocation of another god despite His existence—in other words, transgressing from Him to another.

Or it is an allusion to what the ignorant among the Arab polytheists used to do; they believed that invoking their deities only benefited them on land, whereas the sea belonged to Allah alone, with no partner. Thus, the meaning is invoking Him, the Exalted, in one instance—such as during the hardships of the sea like storms and the like—and invoking another alongside Him in another instance, which is on land. The best of these views is the middle one.

And His saying: وَلا يَقْتُلُونَ النَّفْسَ الَّتِي حَرَّمَ اللهُ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ "and do not kill the soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause," (25:68). That is, they do not kill the human soul whose killing Allah has forbidden in any circumstance, except when the killing is associated with a right (بالحق), such as killing in retribution (قصاصاً) or as a legal punishment (حداً).

And His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَزْنُونَ "and do not commit adultery." (25:68). That is, they do not approach the forbidden private parts. This was common among the Arabs during the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), and Islam was known for prohibiting adultery (الزنا) and wine (الخمر) from the very beginning of its call.

And His saying: وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثاماً "and whoever does that shall encounter the reward of sin." (25:68). The word "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to what was previously mentioned: polytheism, killing an inviolable soul without right, and adultery. "Reward of sin" (الأثام) refers to the sin (الإثم), which is the consequence of the transgression; it is the recompense of punishment that he will encounter on the Day of Resurrection, as mentioned in the following verse.

His saying, the Exalted: يُضاعَفْ لَهُ الْعَذابُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِ مُهاناً "The punishment shall be doubled for him on the Day of Resurrection, and he shall abide therein forever, humiliated," (25:69). This is an explanation of "encountering the reward of sin." His saying وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِ مُهاناً means he shall abide forever in the punishment while humiliation has befallen him.

Abiding forever (الخلود) in punishment for polytheism is beyond doubt. As for abiding forever for killing an inviolable soul and for adultery—both of which are major sins (الكبائر)—the Quran has explicitly mentioned it for both, as well as for consuming usury (الربا). This can be interpreted as the requirement of the nature of the sin itself, as might be understood from the apparent meaning of His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes." (4:48).

Alternatively, "abiding forever" (الخلود) may be interpreted as a long stay, encompassing both the temporary and the eternal. Or His saying وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ "whoever does that" may be interpreted as doing all three together, because the verses, in reality, purify the believers from what the disbelievers were afflicted with, which is the totality of these sins rather than just some.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلاً صالِحاً فَأُوْلئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ اللهُ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ حَسَناتٍ وَكانَ اللهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً "excepting those who repent, attain faith, and act righteously: for such, Allah will replace their evil deeds with good deeds, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful." (25:70). This is an exception from encountering the reward of sin and abiding forever in it. The exception is conditioned upon repentance (التوبة), faith (الإيمان), and performing righteous deeds (العمل الصالح). As for repentance (التوبة) — the act of returning from disobedience, the lowest degree of which is remorse (الندم); if it were not realized, the servant would not be detached from the sin and would remain persistent in it. As for performing righteous deeds, it is that by which repentance is stabilized and becomes sincere (نصوحاً).

As for including faith (الإيمان) in the condition, it indicates that the exception is specifically from polytheism. Thus, the verse pertains to one who committed polytheism, killed, and committed adultery, or to one who committed polytheism whether or not he also committed the aforementioned killing and adultery. As for one who committed killing or adultery without polytheism, the explanation of the ruling regarding his repentance is undertaken by the following verse.

His saying: فأولئك يبدل الله سيئاتهم حسنات "for such, Allah will replace their evil deeds with good deeds" is a consequence of repentance, faith, and righteous deeds. It describes the beautiful effect that follows: that Allah replaces their evil deeds with good deeds.

It has been said regarding its meaning that Allah erases their past sins through repentance and establishes in their place subsequent acts of obedience; thus, He replaces disbelief with faith, killing without right with struggle (الجهاد) and killing with right, and adultery with chastity and modesty.

It has also been said that "evil deeds" (السيئات) and "good deeds" (الحسنات) refer to their dispositions (ملكاتهما) rather than the acts themselves; thus, He replaces the disposition of evil with the disposition of good.

It has also been said that they refer to the punishment and the reward for them, rather than the acts themselves; thus, He replaces the punishment for killing and adultery, for example, with the reward for killing with right and for modesty.

You are well aware that these views involve diverting the speech from its apparent meaning without evidence to support it.

What the apparent meaning of His saying يُبَدِّلُ اللهُ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ حَسَناتٍ suggests—especially since He concluded it with His saying وَكانَ اللهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً—is that every evil deed itself is transformed into a good deed. The evil deed is not the physical substance of the act issued by its doer, which consists of specific movements common to both the evil and the good deed—such as the act of intercourse common to both adultery and marriage, or eating common to both usurping wealth and eating with the owner's permission. Rather, it is the attribute of the act in terms of its conformity to Allah's command or its opposition to it, for example, in terms of how the human being is affected by it and how it is preserved against him, unlike the act which is a collection of fleeting, passing, and perishing movements, or its title which subsists through it and perishes with its perishing.

These evil effects followed by punishment—namely, the evil deeds (السيئات)—remain attached to the human being until he is held accountable for them on the day when the secrets are examined.

Were it not for a taint of wretchedness (الشقوة) and badness in the essence (الذات), no evil deed would proceed from it; for the felicitous essence (الذات السعيدة), pure in every respect, does not produce a filthy evil deed. Thus, evil deeds only attach to an essence that is wretched and wicked in itself, or an essence in which there is a taint of wretchedness and wickedness.

The necessary consequence of this is that if the essence is purified through repentance and becomes wholesome through faith and righteous deeds, thereby transforming into a felicitous essence with no taint of the filth of wretchedness, its necessary effects—which were evil deeds before that—must also transform. Thus, the effects become suited to the essence through forgiveness from Allah and mercy; and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful.

It is to something like this that the allusion may be in His saying: فَأُوْلئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ اللهُ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ حَسَناتٍ وَكانَ اللهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ تابَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً فَإِنَّهُ يَتُوبُ إِلَى اللهِ مَتاباً "And whoever repents and acts righteously indeed turns to Allah with a [true] conversion." (25:71). "Conversion" (المتاب) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) for repentance. The context of the verse suggests it is intended to remove the sense of wonder at the replacement of evil deeds with good deeds by magnifying the matter of repentance—that it is a special return to Allah, the Glorified. Thus, it is not strange that He replaces evil deeds with good deeds, for He is Allah, who does what He wills.

Furthermore, the verse encompasses repentance from all sins, whether they were accompanied by polytheism or were separate from it. The previous verse—as already alluded to—was subtle in its indication regarding the state of sins when they are devoid of polytheism.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ لا يَشْهَدُونَ الزُّورَ وَإِذا مَرُّوا بِاللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا كِراماً "Those who do not give false testimony, and when they encounter vain talk, they pass by it with dignity." (25:72). It is said in Majma' al-Bayan: "The origin of falsehood (الزور) — the embellishment of the void with that which gives the illusion that it is the truth." [End of quote]. Thus, it includes lying and every vain amusement (لهو باطل) such as singing, obscenity, and foul language in a sense. He also said: "It is said: 'So-and-so acted with dignity (تكرم) regarding what shames him' when he keeps himself pure and honors himself from it." [End of quote].

So His saying وَالَّذِينَ لا يَشْهَدُونَ الزُّورَ, if the meaning of "falsehood" (الزور) is lying, then it stands in the place of the cognate accusative (المفعول المطلق), and the estimation is: "they do not testify a false testimony." If the meaning is vain amusement such as singing and the like, then it is a direct object (مفعول به), and the meaning is: "they do not attend the gatherings of the void." The end of the verse suits the second meaning.

And His saying: وَإِذا مَرُّوا بِاللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا كِراماً "and when they encounter vain talk, they pass by it with dignity." Vain talk (اللغو) — those actions and words that are of no account because they do not contain a rational purpose; it encompasses—as has been said—all sins. The meaning of "passing by vain talk" is passing by the people of vain talk while they are engaged in it.

The meaning is: when they pass by the people of vain talk while they are talking vainly, they pass by turning away from them, keeping themselves pure from entering into their company, mixing with them, or sitting with them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِّرُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَمْ يَخِرُّوا عَلَيْها صُمًّا وَعُمْياناً "Those who, when they are reminded of the signs of their Lord, do not fall at them deaf and blind." (25:73). Falling (الخرور) to the ground is to drop onto it, and it seems in the verse to be a metaphor (كناية) for adhering to a thing and being engrossed in it.

The meaning is: those who, when they are reminded of the signs of their Lord—whether wisdom, a goodly exhortation from the Quran, or revelation—do not fall upon them while they are deaf, not hearing, and blind, not seeing. Rather, they reflect upon them and intellectualize them, taking them with insight (بصيرة). Thus, they believe in their wisdom, take heed of their exhortation, and possess insight into their affair and a clear proof from their Lord.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا هَبْ لَنا مِنْ أَزْواجِنا وَذُرِّيَّاتِنا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ وَاجْعَلْنا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِماماً "And those who say, ‘Our Lord! Give us joy in our wives and offspring, and make us foremost of the God-conscious.’" (25:74). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "His eye became cool (قرت عينه)" means it became happy. The Exalted said: كَيْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُها "so that her eye might be comforted" (20:40). And it is said of one who brings joy: "a comfort of the eye" (قرة عين). He said: قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِي وَلَكَ "a comfort of the eye for me and for you" (28:9). And His saying, the Exalted: هَبْ لَنا مِنْ أَزْواجِنا وَذُرِّيَّاتِنا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ. It is said: its origin is from (القر), meaning coldness; so "his eye became cool" is said to mean it became cold and thus healthy. It is also said: because joy has a cold, settled tear, while grief has a hot tear; therefore, it is said of one against whom a prayer is made: "May Allah make his eye hot." It is also said: it is from (القرار), meaning stability; the meaning is that Allah gave him that which settles his eye so it does not look toward anything else. [End of quote].

Their intent in their spouses and offspring being a "comfort of the eyes" for them is that they should bring them joy through obedience to Allah and avoidance of His disobedience. They have no need or desire for anything else, for they are people of truth who do not follow whims.

And His saying: وَاجْعَلْنا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِماماً "and make us foremost of the God-conscious." That is, racing toward good deeds, preceding toward Your mercy, so that others from the God-conscious (المتقين) follow us, as the Exalted said: فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ "so race each other in good works" (2:148), and He said: سابِقُوا إِلى مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ "Take the lead towards forgiveness from your Lord and a paradise" (57:21), and He said: وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ "And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: they are the ones brought near [to Allah]" (56:10-11). It is as if the intent is for them to be a single rank preceding others among the God-conscious; hence, "leader" (إماماً) was brought in the singular form.

Some have said: "leader" (إمام) is among the words applied to both the singular and the plural. It is also said: "leader" (إمام) is the plural of (آم), meaning one who intends (قاصد), like (صيام) is the plural of (صائم). The meaning would then be: "make us intenders for the God-conscious, adhering to them." In the recitation of the People of the House (أهل البيت), it is: "and make for us from the God-conscious a leader."

His saying, the Exalted: أُوْلئِكَ يُجْزَوْنَ الْغُرْفَةَ بِما صَبَرُوا وَيُلَقَّون فِيها تَحِيَّةً وَسَلاماً خالِدِينَ فِيها حَسُنَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقاماً "Those shall be rewarded with the highest station for their patience, and they shall be met therein with greetings and peace, abiding therein forever. Evil it is as an abode and station." (25:75-76). The highest station (الغرفة) — as has been said, a building above a building; thus, it is the high level of a house; it is a metaphor for the high degree in Paradise.
